info.title: Agentic AI Platform - Agent Service API
info.description:
# Overview

Agent Service is part of Trimble AI Agentic Platform. It is a service to manage and interact with the AI agents.

## Getting Started

**PRIOR TO USING THIS API, YOU MUST AGREE TO OUR TERMS OF SERVICE**

The API is protected by OAuth2.0 and requires a valid TID access token in the `Authorization` header. Refer to [TID Authentication Guide](https://developer.trimble.com/docs/authentication) for details.

The Agent Service API requires the following TID token scopes:

- `openid` - OpenID Connect scope for user identification
- `agents` - Use Agent Service

Most of the APIs require the `account_id` claim from the TID JWT token. Make sure you have a proper default account selected in [My Profile](https://myprofile.trimble.com) and/or [My Profile (STAGE)](https://stage.myprofile.trimblecloud.com).
If you don't have any accounts listed under your profile, contact your organization Account Admin.

## Capabilities Overview

- Create and manage AI agents
  - external and internally stored icons. Internally stored icons are automatically normalized to 256x256 center-cropped square WebP format
  - flexible LLM configuration
  - configuration change tracking via agent versioning
  - free-form string tags for agent categorization, discovery, filtering, and organization
- Agent workflows:
  - RAG workflow with one or multiple knowledge base libraries connected
  - Agentic workflows with tools usage
- Tools support
  - [MCP](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/) tools over HTTP streamable transport. Authentication via TID integration. Timeout configuration per [MCP specification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/lifecycle#timeouts): configurable maximum timeout (default 15 minutes, does NOT reset on progress) and 3-minute inter-event timeout (resets when progress notification is received). MCP servers should send progress notifications at least every 2-3 minutes to keep long-running operations alive. Progress is streamed to the client via AG-UI `CustomEvent` with `name="tool_progress"`.
  - Client side (local) tool calls
  - The built-in tools
- Chat with AI agents via [AG UI](https://docs.ag-ui.com/introduction) protocol
- Conversation history
  - history of threads is personal and accessible only by the thread creator
  - The creator loses access to their threads if they lose access to the corresponding agent (e.g. license is revoked)
  - thread titles are by default automatically generated by LLM based on the initial prompt content. Configurable (on agent level) to be the first 100 chars of the initial prompt instead.
  - Creator of the thread can rename it
  - Creator of the thread can delete it, in this case it is wiped immediately without possibility to recover
  - Threads that are not explicitly deleted by the creator are automatically deleted after 2 years.
  - All threads and history are deleted if agent is permanently deleted (not if soft-deleted AKA deactivated)
- Batch retrieval of multiple resources (agents, threads) in a single request for reduced API chattiness
- Search, filtering and sorting for agents and thread history. User can flexibly use these capabilities to find the needed information
  - FIQL syntax filters to narrow down the scope of the search
  - sorting of the results by different criteria to get the most important results on the first page
  - full text fuzzy search
    - for agents name, description
    - for threads by title and message content
    - for runs by message content
  - autocompletion type of search (search as you type)
- Role-based access control (RBAC) defined in following terms:
  - Subjects: TID users, TID applications, IAM accounts, test automation accounts, Trimble employees, licenses provided by AAIP. More to come (EMS licenses, IAM user groups and roles).
  - Roles: `AgentCreator`, `Admin`, `Editor`, `Viewer`
  - Sharable resources:
    - agents
- Usage Quota Enforcement per user per agent with configurable limits and tracking periods
- Analytics reports for agent owners — summary and daily time series data

### Planned Capabilities

- Thread sharing (RBAC for threads)
- File upload in the context of a thread
- Image analysis and generation
- Voice interactions in addition to text interactions

## API versions (v1 vs v2) and migration

The service exposes two URL prefixes: **`/v1`** and **`/v2`**. In OpenAPI, **`/v1` is marked deprecated** to signal that new product work should target **`/v2`**. **`/v1` remains supported** for existing integrations that have not migrated yet.

### What is the same

Both versions implement the same core workflows: **OAuth2 (TID)**, **agents**, **threads**, **runs** (including streaming execution), **reports**, **search-style discovery** (within the paths each version exposes), **tools**, **tags**, and **user profile**. Shared concepts such as **pagination** (`skipToken`, `pageSize`, `links.next`), **`fields` projection**, **`state` for soft-deleted resources**, **FIQL-style filters** where supported, and **RBAC** apply on both prefixes unless a specific endpoint says otherwise.

### What exists only on `/v2`

These capabilities have **no `/v1` equivalent** under the versioned API (clients must use **`/v2`**):

- **Agent configuration lifecycle:** `GET/POST /v2/agents/{agentId}/configs`, `GET/PATCH …/configs/{configId}/metadata`, draft **save** and **discard**, and related schemas (for example **`AgentConfigurationResponse`**, consumer projections on configurations).
- **Published agent alias:** `PATCH /v2/agents/{agentId}/aliases/published` (**`publishAgentV2`**).
- **Agent aliases (additional routes):** alias management under **`/v2/agents/.../aliases`**.
- **Agent audit logs:** `GET /v2/agents/{agentId}/logs` (**`listAgentLogsV2`**).

New product features are expected to land on **`/v2`** first.

### Search and autocomplete (`/v1` vs `/v2`)

Discovery endpoints exist on **both** prefixes; prefer **`/v2`** for new work.

- **`GET /v1/search`** (`operationId`: **`search`**) and **`GET /v1/autocomplete`** (`operationId`: **`autocomplete`**) are part of the **deprecated** v1 surface. Responses use the v1 schema set (for example **`SearchResponse`**, **`AutocompleteResponse`**, and **`AutocompleteItem`** discriminated on `type`).
- **`GET /v2/search`** (`operationId`: **`searchV2`**) and **`GET /v2/autocomplete`** (`operationId`: **`autocompleteV2`**) return **`SearchV2Response`** and **`AutocompleteV2Response`**, with items typed for v2 (for example **`AgentSearchV2Item`** and v2 autocomplete item variants). V2 search supports additional **`fields`** selectors (including v2 agent config paths and **`highlights`**) and an agent **`scope`** query (`latest` / `draft` / `published`) as documented in OpenAPI.

Run hits inside **`GET /v1/search`** use the **flat `RunResponse` / `MessageBase`** payload (same as v1 list-runs). Run hits inside **`GET /v2/search`** use **`RunSearchItemV2`** with **`RunResponseV2`** and the **role-discriminated `MessageResponse`** union, consistent with **`GET /v2/threads/{threadId}/runs`** and **`GET /v2/agents/{agentId}/runs`**.

### Agent and access-control JSON shapes

- **Viewer-role claim list:** On **`/v1`**, request and response bodies use the property name **`users`** for the Viewer-role list. On **`/v2`**, the same concept is exposed as **`viewers`** (see **Access Control Model** in this document).
- **Agent CRUD and wire models:** **`/v1`** agent resources use **`AgentResponse`** / **`AgentUserResponse`** (and related v1 schemas). **`/v2`** list/get/create/update/delete agents use **`AgentV2Response`** and related **`AgentV2*`** schemas (`operationId`s such as **`listAgentsV2`**, **`getAgentV2`**, **`createAgentV2`**, **`updateAgentV2`**, **`deleteAgentV2`**). Do not assume field parity between v1 and v2 agent payloads without checking the OpenAPI components for each version.

### Run history, search hits, and message models

Some **response shapes and schema names** differ so that v2 can offer stricter typing and a clearer contract for generated clients. **Important:** the **role-discriminated `MessageResponse` union** applies to **v2 list-runs**, **v2 agent run listing**, and **run hits in `GET /v2/search`** (**`RunSearchItemV2`** / **`RunResponseV2`**). **`/v1` list-runs**, **v1 agent run listing**, and **run hits in `GET /v1/search`** keep the **flat `MessageBase`** object on **`RunResponse`**.

- **Thread run listing:** `GET /v1/threads/{threadId}/runs` returns **`ListRunsResponse`** whose `items[].messages` are **`MessageBase`** (flat keys, nulls when not applicable). **`GET /v2/threads/{threadId}/runs`** returns **`ListRunsV2Response`** with **`MessageResponse`** (discriminated by `role`).
- **Agent run listing (anonymized history):** `GET /v1/agents/{agentId}/runs` → **`ListAnonymizedRunResponse`** / flat **`MessageBase`**. **`GET /v2/agents/{agentId}/runs`** → **`ListAnonymizedRunV2Response`** / **`MessageResponse`** union.
- **Search run hits:** **`GET /v1/search`** run matches use **flat `RunResponse` / `MessageBase`**. **`GET /v2/search`** run matches use **`RunSearchItemV2`** with **`RunResponseV2`** / **`MessageResponse`**.

### Threads, feedback, batch-get, and `operationId` suffixes

- **Threads:** Listing and CRUD exist on both prefixes; v2 uses suffixed **`operationId`s** (for example **`listThreadsV2`**, **`getThreadV2`**, **`updateThreadV2`**, **`deleteThreadV2`**).
- **Run feedback:** `POST /v1/threads/{threadId}/runs/{runId}/feedback` (**`addRunFeedback`**) vs `POST /v2/threads/{threadId}/runs/{runId}/feedback` (**`addRunFeedbackV2`**).
- **Batch retrieval:** `POST /v1/batch-get` (**`batchGet`**) vs `POST /v2/batch-get` (**`batchGetV2`**); use the schema set that matches the URL prefix you call.
- **Reports, tools, tags, profile:** Shared paths exist on both versions with **distinct `operationId` values** on **`/v2`** (typically a **`V2`** suffix) so a single OpenAPI document can list every operation uniquely. When regenerating clients, scope generated method names to **`/v1` or `/v2`**, not only to the path string.

### OpenAPI tags on v1

Routers mounted under **`/v1`** are included with **deprecated-only** tag metadata (for example **“Agents (v1 - deprecated)”**) so operations appear in the correct Swagger groups. Nested routers do **not** add a second non-deprecated tag for the same routes, avoiding duplicate tag groupings in the published spec.

### Migration guidance

1. **Default new integrations to `/v2`** and regenerate OpenAPI-driven SDKs from the **`/v2`** paths you actually call.
2. **Adopt v2 agent models** (`AgentV2Response`, configuration endpoints, **`viewers`**) when replacing v1 agent management; plan data mapping where JSON property names changed.
3. **Update run-listing and v2 search-run parsing** when moving to v2: use **`ListRunsV2Response`**, **`RunResponseV2`**, **`ListAnonymizedRunV2Response`**, **`RunSearchItemV2`**, and the **`MessageResponse`** union instead of assuming every message includes all nullable keys from **`MessageBase`**.
4. **Keep tolerant parsing** for forward compatibility: ignore unknown JSON properties, and treat unrecognized `role` values or union variants safely (see **Forward Compatibility for Clients** under Common API Concepts).
5. **Stay on `/v1` only while** you still depend on the flat **`MessageBase`** shape on **thread/agent list-runs** and **v1 search run hits**, the legacy **`/v1`** search/autocomplete wire models only, or **`users`**-named viewer lists—and until you migrate those call sites to **`/v2`**.

## Common API Concepts

This API strives to comply with the common [Trimble API Standards](https://developer.trimble.com/docs/api-standard/).

In most cases, this API uses HTTPS requests with JSON-formatted content in requests and responses.
The chat API uses SSE response streaming because of the asynchronous and long-running nature of this API.

For a concise comparison of **`/v1` vs `/v2`**, run listing payloads, and how to migrate, see **API versions (v1 vs v2) and migration** above.

### Cross-Origin Resource Sharing

This API features Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) implemented in compliance with the
[W3C spec](https://www.w3.org/TR/cors/) that allows cross-domain communication from the browser.
All responses have a wildcard same-origin which makes them completely public and accessible to everyone,
including any code on any site.

### Date Format

All dates in the API use UTC and are strings in the [ISO 8601 combined date and time representation
format](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ISO_8601#Combined_date_and_time_representations).
Example: `2025-06-15T15:50:38.311456Z`.

### Eventual Read Consistency

The API responses are eventually consistent.
When you read data from the service, the response might not reflect the results of the most recently completed
write operation. The response might include some stale data due to caching
in place. If you repeat your read request after a short time, the response should return the latest data.

### Forward Compatibility for Clients

Client integrations should follow a "tolerant reader" approach so they keep working as the API evolves.

- Do not assume message roles are a fixed closed set.
- When reading message payloads, ignore/skip message types (`role` values) you do not recognize instead of failing request processing or UI rendering.
- Do not fail on unknown object properties; preserve behavior for known fields and ignore the rest.
- Treat enum-like string fields similarly: if an unknown value appears, handle it gracefully (for example, show a generic fallback in UI) instead of crashing.

This is especially important for run history payloads where new message types may be introduced over time (for example, `activity` or `reasoning`), even if older clients do not yet have first-class support for them.

### Usage Quota Enforcement

Agent creators must configure usage quotas to control resource consumption on a per-user, per-agent basis.

#### Usage Quota Configuration

Each agent must have usage quota limits defined with the following parameters:

- **Runs Limit**: Maximum number of new runs allowed within the tracking period
- **Tokens Limit**: Maximum number of tokens (input + output) allowed to be consumed within the tracking period
- **Tracking Period**: Time window in minutes for quota measurement (e.g., 60 minutes, 1440 minutes for 24 hours)
- **Exclude List**: List of users/applications to exempt from quota enforcement

#### Moving Window Concept

Quotas use a **sliding window** (moving window) approach:

- The tracking period is not a fixed calendar period but a rolling time window
- Usage is calculated from the current time backwards for the specified period duration
- As time progresses, older usage automatically "falls off" the window
- Example: With a 60-minute tracking period, at 3:00 PM, the system counts usage from 2:00 PM to 3:00 PM

#### Quota Enforcement Behavior

When a user attempts to execute a run:

1. The system calculates current usage within the tracking period window
2. The system estimates tokens needed for the current request
3. If either limit would be exceeded:
   - The request is rejected with **HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests)** status code
   - The response includes details about which quota was exceeded
4. Users in the exclude list bypass all quota checks

#### Monitoring Usage

Clients can retrieve current usage statistics via the agent's `usage` field (requires `fields=usage` query parameter).
The usage response includes:

- `prompts`: Current number of runs within the tracking period
- `tokens`: Current token usage within the tracking period
- `trackingPeriod`: Configuration of the time window
- `promptLimit`: Maximum runs allowed
- `tokenLimit`: Maximum tokens allowed

This allows clients to calculate usage percentages and display progress to users before hitting limits.

### Paginated Responses

All endpoints that return a collection of resources follow the same schema for query parameters and response structure to paginate the results. Pagination is cursor-based and client-driven.

| Parameter | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `skipToken` | Opaque cursor for the next page. Omit to request the first page. |
| `pageSize` | Maximum number of items the client wants per page. The server may return fewer items (see below). If omitted, the server returns the maximum possible number of items by default. |
| `links.next.href` | Response property containing the URL to the next page. Absent when there are no more results. |

#### `pageSize` behavior

- The maximum page size is different for each endpoint and specified as part of the endpoint description. It may also vary depending on request parameters (e.g. the `fields` query parameter).
- If `pageSize` exceeds the endpoint's supported maximum, the server returns an error.
- The server may return fewer items than `pageSize` (or even zero) if the response payload would be too large or if some items are not accessible to the requester.
- The server may include `links.next.href` even when the current page contains fewer than `pageSize` items or is empty. Clients should always follow `links.next.href` when it is present to retrieve remaining results.

#### Common Query Parameters

In addition to query parameters (`pageSize`, `skipToken`) used for paging responses, there are several query
parameters that are commonly used across all APIs.

- `state` - Filter by agent state (`active` or `deleted`). Default is `active` only. APIs return 404 when the
resource's state (or, for threads, the associated agent's state) does not match the requested filter. Use
`state=deleted` to access soft-deleted agents or threads from soft-deleted agents; use
`state=active,deleted` to include both. See the Soft and Hard Delete section for details.
- The `fields` query parameter is used to specify the fields to be included in the response. If not provided, a minimal number of fields will be included as default. The `fields` parameter is an array of field names passed as a comma separated array values, e.g. `fields=name,description`. The list of supported fields is defined for each resource type.
- The `sortBy` query parameter is used to specify the order of the items in the response. If not provided, the default order is applied (typically creation timestamp). The `sortBy` parameter is a string that can be one of the predefined values, e.g. `createdAt`, `-createdAt`. The `-` and `+` prefixes are used to specify the order direction (ascending or descending). Default order is ascending if no prefix is specified.

- All identifiers are represented as strings, client should not make assumptions about the format of the identifiers, they are opaque to the client.
- All APIs return `x-request-id` header that contains a unique identifier for the request. This identifier can be used to track the request in the logs and for debugging purposes.

## Data Model

Main resources the API interacts with are `agents` and `threads` (also referred to as conversations).
A thread is a series of `runs`.
Each run is a workflow execution from the initial prompt sent by the user to the final completion created by the LLM. This execution might include several state machine transitions in the workflow.
Each workflow transition is represented as a `message`. The `message` is an interaction primitive that can represent a user prompt, tool call, tool result, or final agent completion.

### Allowed Message Roles in Run Requests

When creating a run (`POST /agents/{agentId}/runs`), the `messages` array only accepts the following roles:

- **`user`** — User prompts and questions.
- **`tool`** — Tool execution results (must reference a preceding tool call via `toolCallId`).
- **`activity`** — Frontend activity signals (e.g., UI state transitions). These are persisted but not forwarded to the model.

Messages with `system`, `developer`, `assistant`, or `reasoning` roles are rejected with HTTP 422. The system prompt and agent personality are controlled exclusively through the agent configuration.

## RAG

The RAG workflow can be enabled by adding a `retrieval` configuration to the agent.

During the workflow execution, the agent can access the knowledge base libraries connected to the agent and use them to answer the user prompts.

The RAG step is a fixed step in the workflow that agent does once per run. The results of this step are exposed as a built-in virtual tool called `search_knowledge_base`. Consumer will see the parameters of the search including the re-written prompt together with retrieved information as a result in a form of AG UI event and part of the conversation history.

The retrieval configuration allows defining a separate prompt to create a meaningful KB search query based on the conversation history and the most recent message in the conversation (prompt re-writing). This KB search query is used to retrieve relevant documents from the knowledge base. If none of the retrieved documents pass the configured score threshold, the agent will be informed that "no sources with enough relevance to the query were found". It is recommended to instruct the agent in the system prompt how to behave in this situation.

## Tools

Agent uses tools to interact with the environment.

Tool types:

- There are two virtual tools: `search_knowledge_base` and `get_run_context` that represent interactions with the RAG sources and run context.
- There are built-in tools that represent agent skills available out of the box and can be enabled for the agent. List of built-in tools is discoverable dynamically via `GET /tools` endpoint.
  - The `datetime` tool makes agent aware about the current date and time
  - The `myprofile` tool retrieves the authenticated user's own profile information. This is useful when the agent needs to personalize responses based on who is asking, or when the user asks questions about their own identity, account, or attributes—such as "Who am I?", "What's my email?", "What account am I in?", "Am I a Trimble employee?", or "What are my roles?".
  The tool returns the following user information: `id`, `email`, `firstName`, `lastName`, `locale`, `timezone`, `countryCode`, `identityType` (user, application, or device), `emailVerified`, `dataRegion`, `pictureUrl`, `account` (with id, name, description), `trimbleEmployee`, `testAutomationUser`, and `roles` (array of role names).
  **Important:** This tool only returns data about the currently logged-in user. To look up information about other users in the organization, use the `user_directory` tool instead.
  - The `user_directory` tool set enables agents to look up users in the same organization or account as the requesting user. This is useful for agents that need to assist with collaboration, delegation, or communication tasks—such as finding the right person to contact for a specific role or project.
  **Important:** Results are scoped to the requesting user's organization or account and respect organizational privacy policies. The tool cannot access users outside the organization boundary.
  The `user_directory` tool set provides three operations:
    - `list_users_in_organization`: Searches the organization's user directory by partial name or email, or lists all users when no query is provided. An organization may have several accounts, so this tool has a broader scope than `list_users_in_account`. Only available to Trimble employees. Returns: `id`, `email`, `first_name`, `last_name`, `language`, `picture`, `country`, `status`, `timezone`, `default_account`, `last_login_time_stamp`.
    - `list_users_in_account`: Searches the user's account directory by partial name or email, or lists all users in the account. Use when looking up users in the same account or business unit. Returns: `id`, `email`, `first_name`, `last_name`, `language`, `picture`, `country`, `status`, `timezone`, `default_account`, `last_login_time_stamp`.
    - `get_user_by_id`: Retrieves a specific user's profile by their unique ID. Returns: `id`, `email`, `first_name`, `last_name`, `language`, `picture`, `country`, `status`, `timezone`, `default_account`, `last_login_time_stamp`.
  - The `tekla_help` tool allows to search [Tekla User Assistance](https://support.tekla.com/) for user help and support articles on Tekla products. The tool allows additional configuration of the http headers with substitution, search index name prefix and url as part of the agent configuration.
  Default configuration:

  ```json
  {
    "type": "tekla_help",
    "url": "https://api.search.cloud.tekla.com/query/v1.0",
    "indexPrefix": "tua_prod-",
    "headers": [
      { "name": "Authorization", "value": "IdToken None" },
      { "name": "Content-Type", "value": "application/json" }
    ]
  }
  ```

  - The `trimble_help` tool allows to search [Trimble Help](https://help.trimble.com/) for user help and support articles on Trimble products. The tool allows additional configuration of the http headers with substitution, search index name prefix and url as part of the agent configuration.
  Default configuration:

  ```json
  {
    "type": "trimble_help",
    "url": "https://search.trimble.com/api/v1.0",
    "indexPrefix": "thlp_prod-",
    "headers": [
      { "name": "Authorization", "value": "IdToken None" },
      { "name": "Content-Type", "value": "application/json" }
    ]
  }
  ```

  Headers override example:

  ```json
  {
    "type": "trimble_help",
    "headers": [
      { "name": "Authorization", "value": "Bearer {actorTokenRaw}" },
      { "name": "X-Custom-Header", "value": "{run.context.<description>}" },
      { "name": "X-App-Id", "value": "1234567890" }
    ]
  }
  ```

  - The `web_search` tool allows agents to perform web searches and retrieve results with title, URL, and snippet for each result.
  - The `curl` tool allows agents to call arbitrary HTTP APIs, similar to the `curl` command line utility. Tool call arguments accepted by the model: `method` (HTTP method), `url` (absolute http(s) URL), optional `query` (object of query parameters), `headers` (array of `{name, value}`), `body` (raw string) or `jsonBody` (JSON value), and `timeoutSeconds` (1–120, default 30). Relative URLs are not supported — the model must always supply the full URL.

    Agent-level configuration fields:
    - `allowedBaseUrls` (optional, **security guardrail**) — a list of scheme+host+port+path URL prefixes the tool is allowed to call. When set and non-empty, every tool-call URL must match at least one entry; otherwise the call is rejected with a structured `{"status": "error", "message": "URL not allowed by agent configuration: ..."}` result before any DNS resolution or network I/O. When unset or empty, the tool may call any public http(s) endpoint permitted by the service-wide SSRF guard. Each entry:
      - must have an `http` or `https` scheme and a host;
      - must NOT contain a query string, fragment, userinfo (`user:pass@`), or `.` / `..` path segments (rejected at config time);
      - is compared to tool-call URLs using: case-insensitive scheme+host, port after applying protocol defaults (so `https://example.com/x` and `https://example.com:443/x` are equivalent), and path-segment boundary prefix match on the path (`/v1` admits `/v1` and `/v1/anything` but NOT `/v1foo`);
      - matches are performed against the tool-call URL *after* its path is normalized (collapsing `.` / `..` / duplicate `/` segments), so a call like `https://host/v1/../admin` cannot use path traversal to escape an allowed `/v1` prefix — it normalizes to `https://host/admin` and is rejected.

      Best practice for production agents: set `allowedBaseUrls` to the smallest set of upstream prefixes the agent needs (ideally the exact path under which the API lives). This limits the blast radius if the agent is prompt-injected into calling an unintended endpoint.
    - `headers` — static HTTP headers attached to every curl tool call. Header values support placeholder substitution: `{actorToken?scopes=...}`, `{actorTokenRaw}`, and `{run.context.<description>}`. Unlike MCP tools, the curl tool deliberately does NOT accept `{agentToken?scopes=...}` — because the curl tool can target arbitrary HTTP URLs, injecting an agent (client credentials) token would let a prompt-injected model reach any upstream endpoint that trusts the agent beyond what the original actor authorized. Configure an MCP tool if agent-as-itself authentication is required.

    Authentication is configured similarly to MCP tools: add an `Authorization` header (or any other header the upstream API expects) to the agent-level `headers` and use an actor-token placeholder for its value. The placeholder is resolved at tool-call time.
    - `{ "name": "Authorization", "value": "Bearer {actorToken?scopes=<scopes>}" }` — on-behalf-of token derived from the original actor token via the TID Token Exchange flow. Recommended for calls that should act on behalf of the end user.
    - Caller-supplied `headers` in a tool call override agent-level headers with the same name (case-insensitive), so an individual call can bypass the configured `Authorization` header when needed.

    Response behavior: the full HTTP response status, reason, final URL, headers, and body are returned to the model. Text responses (e.g. JSON, XML, HTML, plain text) are returned as-is in the `content` field. Binary responses are returned base64-encoded with `"contentEncoding": "base64"`. Response content is **not** truncated — the API response body is returned unchanged, up to a hard per-call cap of **10 MiB**. Responses whose body exceeds the cap (either by declared `Content-Length` or by streamed size) are rejected with a structured `{"status": "error", "message": ...}` result rather than returning a partial/corrupted payload. This cap protects the service from memory exhaustion when the tool is pointed at endpoints that return very large payloads.

    Header sanitization: the model never sees the tokens or secrets carried by HTTP headers on either side of the call — neither on the request side (agent-injected credentials) nor on the response side (credentials issued by the upstream API). Concretely:

    - **Request side — agent-injected credentials are invisible to the model.** Token placeholders in agent-level `headers` (`{actorToken?scopes=...}`, `{actorTokenRaw}`, `{run.context.<description>}`) are resolved *inside the service at runtime*, **after** the model has generated its tool-call arguments and **outside** the model's tool call itself. The resolved secret values are held briefly in memory on the tool instance, merged with the caller-supplied headers, and written directly onto the outgoing HTTP request to the upstream API. They are not echoed back to the model in the tool result, are not written to logs, and are not persisted anywhere in the run's conversation history. The model only ever sees the placeholder form of the header value (if it sees the agent config at all) — never the resolved token. This property holds even if the model is prompt-injected: because the model never authored the header, it cannot read it back out of its own tool call.

      Caller-supplied headers in the tool call (which the model itself writes into the `headers` argument) **are** visible to the model and are stored verbatim in the run's conversation history — that is just the model's own output being persisted. Do not rely on the model to pass secrets through its own `headers` argument; configure them as agent-level `headers` with a token placeholder instead, so the secret is injected at runtime and the model never authors it.
    - **Response side — credentials issued by the upstream are redacted.** Credential-bearing response headers have their values replaced with the sentinel string `"<redacted>"` before the response is returned to the model. This prevents secrets issued by upstream APIs from leaking into the run's conversation history (which is visible to agent admins and editors) and blocks a class of exfiltration attack where a prompt-injected model is coaxed into echoing captured credentials back to the caller. The header *name* is preserved so the model can still reason about the fact that the server issued, for example, a `Set-Cookie`. The redacted header set is: `Authorization`, `Proxy-Authorization`, `Cookie`, `Set-Cookie`, `X-Api-Key`, `Api-Key`, `Apikey`, `X-Auth-Token`, `X-Access-Token`, `X-Session-Token`, `X-Subject-Token`, `X-Csrf-Token`, `X-Xsrf-Token`, `X-Amz-Security-Token`, `X-Goog-Api-Key`, `X-Subscription-Key`. `WWW-Authenticate` / `Proxy-Authenticate` are deliberately **not** redacted — they are server-to-client auth challenges (scheme, realm, optional nonce) and are often necessary for the model to respond appropriately.

    Scope note: response URLs and response bodies are **not** sanitized. If the upstream API echoes a token into the response body (e.g. as a field in a JSON login response), the tool has no reliable way to detect and redact it across arbitrary JSON/XML/HTML/binary payloads, and the body will be visible to the model and persisted in the run. When configuring a curl tool against an upstream that returns tokens in the body, treat that upstream as trusted with the actor's conversation history, or avoid exposing those endpoints via this tool.

    Security: the same SSRF guard as `url_context` applies — calls to `localhost`, private IP ranges, and non-http(s) schemes are blocked, and the outbound connection is pinned to the exact set of IP addresses that passed validation so a malicious authoritative DNS server cannot return a public IP at validation time and a private/internal IP at connection time (DNS rebinding / TOCTOU). HTTP redirects are **never** followed automatically. The tool returns redirect responses (3xx with `Location`) as is; the model must issue a new tool call against the redirected URL if it wants to follow it, and that new URL is re-validated (and re-pinned) by the SSRF guard.

    Minimal configuration:

    ```json
    {
      "type": "curl"
    }
    ```

    Full configuration with an allow-list, on-behalf-of token injection, and substituted headers:

    ```json
    {
      "type": "curl",
      "allowedBaseUrls": [
        "https://agent-service.trimble.com/v1/",
        "https://agent-service.trimble.com/v2/"
      ],
      "headers": [
        { "name": "Authorization", "value": "Bearer {actorToken?scopes=agents openid}" },
        { "name": "X-App-Id", "value": "1234567890" },
        { "name": "X-Context-Product", "value": "{run.context.product}" }
      ]
    }
    ```

- Finally there are external tools that can be connected to the agent: local and MCP.
Both local tools and MCP tools can be used in the agent workflow. Server-side tools are configured in the agent configuration, while local tools can be either also configured in the agent configuration or passed to the run as part of the request.

**Important:** MCP tool result handling of tools that yield results in any other format than [unstructured Text Content](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/server/tools#text-content) is currently in **experimental** phase and is subject to change.

Note that the order in which tool results are saved to a run's conversation history is not guaranteed to be the same as the order in which the LLM called the tools nor the order in which the tool results were streamed to the client.

## MCP Tool Overrides

Tool Schema Overrides give you granular control over how agents interact with MCP tools. While system prompts can guide an agent's behavior, LLMs can sometimes ignore or creatively reinterpret instructions when they conflict with the underlying tool descriptions. Tool Schema Overrides solve this by modifying the tool's signature — including its description and input schema — before it reaches the LLM, ensuring higher reliability and alignment with your business logic.

Key capabilities:

- **Description overrides** — rewrite tool descriptions to provide clearer context or specific instructions to the model.
- **Input schema constraints** — deep-merge [JSON Schema](https://json-schema.org/) fragments into the original tool's input schema to restrict parameters to specific values, enums, or ranges (e.g. locking an agent to a specific Knowledge Library ID).
- **Selective tool access** — use `toolDefaults` to disable all tools from an MCP server by default and explicitly enable only the ones required for your use case.

> **Warning — advanced configuration.** Tool Schema Overrides require a solid understanding of [MCP tool schemas](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-06-18/server/tools) and [JSON Schema](https://json-schema.org/). Misconfigured overrides can modify a tool definition to the extent that the agent will no longer be able to use it correctly — for example, by making required parameters unreachable or by setting contradictory constraints. Use the **`POST /{agentId}/validate`** endpoint to debug and verify tool override configurations before deploying your agent.

### Configuration structure

Tool overrides are specified within an MCP tool entry in the agent's `tools` array:

- **`toolDefaults.allowed`** (`boolean`) — sets the default `allowed` state for all tools exposed by the MCP server. Set to `false` to disable all tools by default, then selectively enable specific tools.
- **`tools`** (array) — per-tool configuration entries, each with:
  - **`name`** (`string`) — the exact tool name as exposed by the MCP server.
  - **`allowed`** (`boolean`) — whether this specific tool is enabled for the agent.
  - **`overrides`** (object) — schema modifications applied via recursive deep merge (see [Merge semantics](#merge-semantics) below):
    - **`description`** (`string`) — replacement description the LLM will see instead of the original.
    - **`inputSchema`** (object) — JSON Schema fragment that is deep-merged into the tool's original input schema.

### Merge semantics

The `inputSchema` override is applied using a **recursive deep merge**, not [JSON Merge Patch (RFC 7396)](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc7396). The key differences:

| Behavior | Recursive deep merge (actual) | JSON Merge Patch (RFC 7396) |
|---|---|---|
| Nested objects | Merged recursively | Merged recursively |
| Scalars & arrays | Overwritten by the override value | Overwritten by the override value |
| `null` values | Set the key to `null` | **Delete** the key from the target |

In practice this means:

- **Object values** (dicts) in the override are recursively merged into the corresponding base key. Existing sibling keys that are not mentioned in the override are preserved.
- **Non-object values** — strings, numbers, booleans, arrays, and `null` — replace the base value wholesale.
- **Setting a key to `null`** stores `null` in the merged result; it does **not** remove the key. This is intentional: JSON Schema uses `null` as a meaningful value (e.g. `"default": null`), so RFC 7396 null-deletion semantics would make certain valid overrides impossible.

### Example: Connecting to a specific Knowledge Library

The following configuration connects an agent to the Knowledge Base MCP server, disables all tools by default, and enables only the `Search` tool — locked to a single library by overriding the `libraryIds` parameter with a fixed enum value:

```json
{
  "tools": [
    {
      "type": "MCP",
      "name": "Knowledge service MCP",
      "url": "{{KB_URL}}/v1/mcp",
      "headers": [
        {
          "name": "Authorization",
          "value": "Bearer {actorToken?scopes=kb}"
        }
      ],
      "toolDefaults": {
        "allowed": false
      },
      "tools": [
        {
          "name": "Search",
          "allowed": true,
          "overrides": {
            "description": "Find relevant information in a library using semantic search.",
            "inputSchema": {
              "properties": {
                "libraryIds": {
                  "items": {
                    "type": "string",
                    "enum": [
                      "1234567890"
                    ]
                  },
                  "minItems": 1,
                  "maxItems": 1
                }
              },
              "required": [
                "query",
                "libraryIds",
                "top"
              ]
            }
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

In this example:

- `toolDefaults.allowed: false` disables every tool the MCP server exposes.
- The `Search` tool is explicitly re-enabled with `allowed: true`.
- The `description` override replaces the original tool description with a more specific instruction.
- The `inputSchema` override constrains `libraryIds` to exactly one value (`"1234567890"`) using an `enum` with `minItems`/`maxItems` set to `1`, preventing the LLM from passing arbitrary library IDs.
- The `required` array ensures the LLM always passes `query`, `libraryIds`, and `top`.

### Example: Connecting to several Knowledge Libraries

When the agent needs to search across multiple knowledge libraries simultaneously, use the same override pattern with an expanded enum and adjusted cardinality:

```jsonc
{
  "tools": [
    {
      "type": "MCP",
      "name": "Knowledge service MCP",
      "url": "{{KB_URL}}/v1/mcp",
      "headers": [
        {
          "name": "Authorization",
          "value": "Bearer {actorToken?scopes=kb}"
        }
      ],
      "toolDefaults": {
        "allowed": false
      },
      "tools": [
        {
          "name": "Search",
          "allowed": true,
          "overrides": {
            "description": "Find grounding information about ....",
            "inputSchema": {
              "properties": {
                "libraryIds": {
                  "description": "Always pass these ids: guid1, guid2, guid3.",
                  "type": "array",
                  "items": {
                    "type": "string",
                    "enum": [
                      "guid1",
                      "guid2",
                      "guid3"
                    ]
                  },
                  "minItems": 3,
                  "maxItems": 3
                }
              }
            }
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

In this example:

- The `enum` lists all three library GUIDs the agent is allowed to search.
- `minItems: 3` and `maxItems: 3` force the LLM to always include all three libraries in every search call.
- The `description` on `libraryIds` provides an explicit instruction to the LLM listing the expected IDs.

## Subagents

Subagents allow an agent to delegate work to other agents.
Subagent are modeled as tool calls from the parent agent perspective - parent agent's LLM decides when to call them and what tasks to delegate.
Each subagent is running asynchronously, parent agent can decide to run several subagents in parallel.
Subagents are run in the isolated remote independent context, not blocking the parent agent. This model is optimized for long running tasks rather than short-lived (seconds) tasks.
Subagents are stateful (follow normal thread-run model) so parent agent can continue conversation with the subagent and send follow-up instructions or course-correct mid-task.
Parent agent is using AG UI protocol to communicate with remote subagents via HTTP streaming transport, acting as a mediator for subagent communication with end users.

**Context window optimization:** Subagents provide an efficient method to overcome context window size limitations. By dividing complex tasks into subtasks, each subagent starts from a clean context and receives only the relevant summary from the parent agent as input. This keeps each agent's context focused and within token limits, enabling complex multi-step workflows that would otherwise exceed a single agent's context capacity.

**Configuration:** The `subagents` array on the agent configuration accepts objects with:

- `id` — the target agent ID to delegate to
- `configId` (optional) — a specific published configuration version. If omitted, `configs.latest` is used at runtime
- `overrides` (optional) — `name` and/or `description` overrides for the tool the LLM sees

**Tool name derivation:** Each subagent is presented to the parent LLM as a tool. The tool name is derived from the subagent's agent name (or the `overrides.name` if provided) by replacing every character outside `[a-zA-Z0-9_-]` with an underscore, collapsing consecutive underscores, and stripping leading/trailing underscores. For example, `"My Research Agent"` becomes `My_Research_Agent`. If the name consists entirely of characters that are removed during sanitization (e.g. non-ASCII / Unicode-only names), the service falls back to `subagent_<agentId>`. Naming recommendations:

- Use ASCII letters, digits, hyphens, and underscores in subagent names for predictable tool names.
- Keep names short and descriptive — the LLM uses the tool name to decide when to delegate.
- When the subagent's own name is not suitable, set `overrides.name` to a clear, ASCII-friendly identifier such as `"code-reviewer"` or `"data_analyst"`.
- Avoid names that differ only by non-ASCII characters, as they may produce identical tool names after sanitization, causing one to shadow the other.

**Runtime behavior:** Each subagent invocation creates a separate thread and run, linked to the parent via `parentRunId`. The subagent starts from a clean context — it does not inherit the parent's conversation history. The parent's `forwardedProps` are automatically passed through to the subagent.

**Access control:** The parent agent's editor can add subagent even without having access to the subagent, no access validation is performed at agent configuration time. The validate endpoint can be used to check subagent reachability.
The access to the subagent is guarded at the execution time - actor must have an an appropriate access to subagent in order to execute it.

**Recursion limits:** Maximum delegation depth of 3. Otherwise there are no limits in agents dependencies: agent can call itself or subagent can call parent agent as subagent (agent swarm).

**Context variable substitution caution:** Subagents that use `{run.context.<description>}` variable substitution in their tool headers or retrieval connection strings depend on receiving the correct context entries from the parent agent's LLM. Since the LLM decides what context to pass to the subagent, this behavior is non-deterministic. Recommendations:

- Clearly state required context entries in the subagent's description (or in the `overrides.description`).
- Keep context variable requirements minimal in subagent configurations.
- Prefer passing necessary information directly in the message content rather than relying on context variables.

**Validation:** The `POST /{agentId}/validate?checks=subagents` endpoint verifies subagent reachability, access, and configuration compatibility (including local tools check). Set `applyOverrides=true` to see the resolved name and description as the LLM would see them.

**Local tools limitation:** Subagents cannot have local (client-side) tools configured. Local tools use an interrupt mechanism that requires a frontend client to handle the tool execution. When a parent agent delegates to a subagent, the subagent runs as a server-side HTTP call with no frontend client attached, making local tool interrupts unresolvable. Use the validate endpoint to detect this misconfiguration.

**Streaming — Subagent Event Relay:**

When a parent agent delegates to a subagent, all AG-UI events produced by the subagent are forwarded to the parent's SSE stream in real time as AG-UI `CustomEvent` messages. Each relayed event carries a `SUBAGENT_`-prefixed name and a structured `value` envelope that identifies the subagent that produced the event.

**Naming convention:** Every relayed event uses the name `SUBAGENT_<OriginalEventType>`:

| Original AG-UI Event | Relayed `CustomEvent` Name |
| --- | --- |
| `RUN_STARTED` | `CUSTOM` event with name=`SUBAGENT_RUN_STARTED` |
| `RUN_FINISHED` | `CUSTOM` event with name=`SUBAGENT_RUN_FINISHED` |
| `RUN_ERROR` | `CUSTOM` event with name=`SUBAGENT_RUN_ERROR` |
| `TEXT_MESSAGE_START` | `CUSTOM` event with name=`SUBAGENT_TEXT_MESSAGE_START` |
| `TEXT_MESSAGE_CONTENT` | `CUSTOM` event with name=`SUBAGENT_TEXT_MESSAGE_CONTENT` |
| `TEXT_MESSAGE_END` | `CUSTOM` event with name=`SUBAGENT_TEXT_MESSAGE_END` |
| `TOOL_CALL_START` | `CUSTOM` event with name=`SUBAGENT_TOOL_CALL_START` |
| `TOOL_CALL_ARGS` | `CUSTOM` event with name=`SUBAGENT_TOOL_CALL_ARGS` |
| `TOOL_CALL_END` | `CUSTOM` event with name=`SUBAGENT_TOOL_CALL_END` |
| `TOOL_CALL_RESULT` | `CUSTOM` event with name=`SUBAGENT_TOOL_CALL_RESULT` |
| `CUSTOM` (name starts with `SUBAGENT_`) | `CUSTOM` event keep origin name (single `SUBAGENT_` prefix preserved) |
| `CUSTOM` (name other than `SUBAGENT_`) | `CUSTOM` event with name=`SUBAGENT_CUSTOM` |

**Envelope `value` schema:**

| Field | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `agentId` | string | The subagent's agent ID at this delegation level |
| `threadId` | string | The subagent's thread ID (from its `RUN_STARTED`) |
| `runId` | string | The subagent's run ID (from its `RUN_STARTED`) |
| `toolCallId` | string | The parent's tool call ID that invoked this subagent — correlate with the corresponding `TOOL_CALL_START` event |
| `event` | object | The original AG-UI event as produced by the subagent |

**Depth-1 wire format example** (root → child):

```json
{
  "type": "CUSTOM",
  "name": "SUBAGENT_TEXT_MESSAGE_CONTENT",
  "value": {
    "agentId": "child-agent-id",
    "threadId": "child-thread-id",
    "runId": "child-run-id",
    "toolCallId": "root-tool-call-id",
    "event": {
      "type": "TEXT_MESSAGE_CONTENT",
      "delta": "Hello from the child agent",
      "messageId": "msg-1"
    }
  }
}
```

**Depth-2 wire format example** (root → child → grandchild):

When the child agent itself delegates to a grandchild, the grandchild's events arrive at the root with two layers of nesting. The child wraps the grandchild's already-relayed `SUBAGENT_*` events in its own envelope. The outermost `name` stays single-prefixed based on the innermost original event type — prefixes never stack.

```json
{
  "type": "CUSTOM",
  "name": "SUBAGENT_TEXT_MESSAGE_CONTENT",
  "value": {
    "agentId": "child-agent-id",
    "threadId": "child-thread-id",
    "runId": "child-run-id",
    "toolCallId": "root-tool-call-id",
    "event": {
      "type": "CUSTOM",
      "name": "SUBAGENT_TEXT_MESSAGE_CONTENT",
      "value": {
        "agentId": "grandchild-agent-id",
        "threadId": "grandchild-thread-id",
        "runId": "grandchild-run-id",
        "toolCallId": "child-tool-call-id",
        "event": {
          "type": "TEXT_MESSAGE_CONTENT",
          "delta": "Hello from the grandchild",
          "messageId": "gc-msg-1"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

**Transient semantics:** Relayed events are never persisted in the parent's conversation history or passed to the LLM context. They are best-effort — if the internal relay queue is full, events are silently dropped. The `TOOL_CALL_RESULT` event (containing the subagent's complete text output) remains the authoritative result path and is always delivered.

**Client parsing guidance:** To walk the nesting and extract events at every delegation level, unwrap recursively:

```javascript
function* unwrapSubagentEvent(value) {
  yield { agentId: value.agentId, event: value.event }
  if (value.event.type === "CUSTOM"
      && value.event.name?.startsWith("SUBAGENT_")) {
    yield* unwrapSubagentEvent(value.event.value)
  }
}
```

**Extensibility:** The relay currently covers the event types listed above. Future versions may extend relay coverage to additional event types (e.g., local tool calls) using the same envelope structure.

## Agent Tags

Tags enable agent discovery by letting users efficiently filter agents in listing and search endpoints. Tags can represent agent categories (e.g., `hr`, `finance`), capabilities (e.g., `rag`, `code-review`), organizational groupings, or any free-form classification the agent editor finds useful. Tag matching is **case-sensitive** — `Production` and `production` are distinct values.

Tags are part of the agent configuration and are version-specific. Filtering, listing, and autocomplete operate on the tags from the latest saved version of each agent's configuration (`configs.latest`). When a new configuration version is saved, the tag set from that version replaces the previous one for discovery purposes.

To help agent editors select appropriate tags, the service provides:

- `GET /tags` — Lists all tags currently in use across agents accessible to the caller, with usage counts, sorted by name or usage.
- Autocomplete endpoint — Returns matching tags ranked by relevance for typeahead/search-as-you-type UI.

The list of available tags is **crowdsourced automatically** from tags applied to agents. There is no central tag registry — any tag value an editor applies to an agent becomes discoverable via these endpoints. If a tag is no longer used on any agent, it is automatically removed from the list within a few hours (the reconciliation job runs every 3 hours).

## Run Context

The concept of the `run context` is used to pass a structured information to the agent during the workflow execution. The run context is a description-value map and part of AG UI protocol. Agent has access to the information in the context using a built-in virtual tool `get_run_context` and can use it to answer user prompts.
E.g. the context can contain the information about product with version that should be used to filter the information retrieved from the knowledge base.
The context value is not suitable for passing secrets, tokens, or other sensitive data.

**Important:** Each context item must have a unique `description` field. Duplicate descriptions are not allowed as they would cause conflicts during placeholder substitution in tool configurations and retrieval connection strings.

## Variable Substitution

In retrieval connection strings and tool configurations, the [format string syntax](https://docs.python.org/3/library/string.html#format-string-syntax) field substitution is used to pass context information to the agent.
Use `{ }` syntax for field substitutions. Escape any other `{` or `}` characters by doubling them, e.g. `{{` or `}}`.

### Supported placeholders by context

| Context | Supported placeholders |
| --- | --- |
| Retrieval connection string | `{run.context.<description>}` |
| MCP tool headers | `{run.context.<description>}`, `{actorTokenRaw}`, `{actorToken?scopes=...}`, `{agentToken?scopes=...}` |
| Query rewriter prompt | `{chat_history}`, `{question}` |
| System prompt | None (not supported for security and caching reasons) |

### Placeholder reference

| Placeholder | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `{run.context.<description>}` | Context variable value from the run request. |
| `{actorTokenRaw}` | Token of the original initiator of the run request. **Not recommended** — the token can expire unpredictably, preventing successful tool execution, and has problems with agent identity propagation. |
| `{actorToken?scopes=...}` | On-behalf token derived from the original request initiator's token. At least one scope must be specified. |
| `{agentToken?scopes=...}` | Agent (client credentials) token created using service app credentials. At least one scope must be specified. |
| `{chat_history}` | Conversation history (managed by token-based context window trimming by default; respects the deprecated `shortMemorySize` run-count cap when set). |
| `{question}` | The most recent message in the thread. |

### Retrieval connection string example

Dynamic metadata filtering in the knowledge base search based on run request context. A connection string can include a filter that references a context variable:

`https://kb.example.com/v1/search?libraries=libId&searchType=semantic&filter=tags==(category:{run.context.category})`

When the run request includes `context: [{description: "category", value: "movies"}]`, the filter is resolved to `tags==(category:movies)` before the knowledge base search is executed. If a referenced context variable is not provided in the run request, the request fails with a `tag:MissingContext` error (HTTP 400).

### System prompt substitution

Substitutions (e.g. `{run.context.<description>}`) are NOT supported in the system prompt for security reasons and to enable caching of the prompt. The run context is provided to the model as the result of a virtual `get_run_context` tool call that appears in the message history. The system prompt can instruct the model to look for and use the data returned by the `get_run_context` tool call when it needs run-specific context (for example, product, category, or other metadata).

## Context Window Management

The service automatically manages how conversation history fills the LLM context window. The strategy depends on the `shortMemorySize` setting in the agent configuration.

**Token-based trimming (default, recommended)** — When `shortMemorySize` is `null` (the default for new agents), the service calculates a token budget for conversation history based on the configured model's context window:

1. Start with the model's maximum context window (e.g. 128 000 tokens for gpt-4o).
2. Subtract `maxOutputTokens` (tokens reserved for the model's response).
3. Multiply the remainder by a 70% utilization ratio to leave headroom.
4. Subtract the tokens used by the system prompt, the current run's messages (user question), and `get_run_context` tool output.
5. The remaining budget is available for conversation history. History is trimmed from the oldest messages so that the most recent interactions are preserved.

This approach ensures the conversation history is always sized appropriately for the model being used, without manual tuning.

**Message priority** — The following elements are never trimmed and are always present in the context window:

- System prompt
- Current run messages (the user's question and any tool calls or results from the current run)
- `get_run_context` tool output (when run context is provided)
- Knowledge base (RAG) search results (when retrieval is configured)

Only conversation history from previous runs is eligible for trimming.

**Legacy run-count trimming (deprecated)** — When `shortMemorySize` is set to an integer, the service limits the number of recent runs loaded from the thread. This approach does not account for the actual token cost of messages and may underutilize or exceed the model's context window. It is retained for backward compatibility but new agents should use the default token-based strategy.

## Token Usage vs. Context Utilization

The run API distinguishes between two related but different metrics:

**Token usage** (`usage`) — The cumulative token consumption across *all* LLM calls in a run. When a run involves tool-call loops or is resumed, each LLM invocation adds to the running total of `promptTokens`, `completionTokens`, and `embedTokens`. This is the number used for billing, quotas, and reporting aggregation.

**Context utilization** (`contextUtilization`) — A snapshot from the *last* LLM invocation only. It describes how much of the model's context window was occupied in that final call:

| Field | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `maxTokens` | integer | The model's total context window size (in tokens). |
| `utilizedTokens` | integer | Tokens consumed in the last LLM call (`input_tokens + output_tokens`). |

For a single-call run without tool calls these two metrics coincide, but for multi-step runs (tool use, resumes) they diverge: `usage` grows with every call while `contextUtilization.utilizedTokens` reflects only the final invocation.

Both fields are optional on the run response and must be requested via the `fields` query parameter (`usage`, `contextUtilization`).

## AG-UI Event Extensions

The Agent Service streams run events using the [AG-UI protocol](https://docs.ag-ui.com/introduction). Several standard AG-UI events carry additional service-specific properties, and `CustomEvent` is used to stream MCP tool progress and subagent output.

### Extended `RUN_STARTED` Event

The `RUN_STARTED` event includes all [standard AG-UI fields](https://docs.ag-ui.com/sdk/js/events#runstarted) (`type`, `threadId`, `runId`, `parentRunId`) plus the following:

| Field | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `agentVersion` | integer | The agent configuration version used for this run. |
| `configId` | string | The resolved configuration snapshot identifier, if the agent uses published configs. |

### Extended `RUN_FINISHED` Event

The `RUN_FINISHED` event includes all [standard AG-UI fields](https://docs.ag-ui.com/sdk/js/events#runfinished) (`type`, `threadId`, `runId`) plus the following:

| Field | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `agentVersion` | integer | The agent configuration version used for this run (same value as in `RUN_STARTED`). |
| `configId` | string | The resolved configuration snapshot identifier (same value as in `RUN_STARTED`). |
| `maxTokens` | integer | The model's context window size (in tokens). |
| `utilizedTokens` | integer | Tokens consumed in the *last* LLM call (input + output). This is the context utilization, not the accumulated total. |
| `contextWindow` | integer | **Deprecated** — use `maxTokens`. Mirrors `maxTokens` for backward compatibility; will be removed in a future release. |
| `usedTokens` | integer | **Deprecated** — use `utilizedTokens`. Mirrors `utilizedTokens` for backward compatibility; will be removed in a future release. Note: the semantics changed together with the rename — this field now reports tokens from the last LLM call only, no longer the accumulated total. |

### Extended `RUN_ERROR` Event

The `RUN_ERROR` event includes the standard `message` and `code` fields plus context utilization when available:

| Field | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `maxTokens` | integer | The model's context window size (in tokens). `0` if the model was not resolved before the error. |
| `utilizedTokens` | integer | Tokens consumed in the last LLM call before the error. `0` if no LLM call occurred. |

### Custom Events

The AG-UI `CUSTOM` event type (`CustomEvent`) carries a `name` string and a free-form `value` object. The service emits the following custom events.

#### MCP Tool Progress — `name="tool_progress"`

Streamed while an MCP tool is executing. MCP servers report progress via the [MCP progress notification](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-11-25/basic/lifecycle#progress) mechanism; the service relays each notification to the client as a custom event. This allows UIs to display progress bars or status messages for long-running tool calls.

**`value` payload:**

| Field | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `progress` | number | Current progress value (e.g., 5 out of 10). |
| `total` | number \| null | Total progress value when known (e.g., 10). `null` if the total is indeterminate. |
| `message` | string \| null | Optional human-readable progress message from the MCP server. |
| `tool_name` | string \| null | Name of the MCP tool being executed. |

**Example SSE payload:**

```json
{
  "type": "CUSTOM",
  "name": "tool_progress",
  "value": {
    "progress": 3,
    "total": 10,
    "message": "Processing page 3 of 10…",
    "tool_name": "index_documents"
  }
}
```

#### Subagent Notifications Streaming

Subagents progress can be observed in real time using the notifications that are streamed as CUSTOM AG UI events.
These notifications are delivered with best effort and can be dropped if service is overloaded. The final complete text is guaranteed to be delivered as a standard `TOOL_CALL_RESULT` event after the subagent finishes.
For more details about subagent notifications refer to [Subagents](#subagents) section of this document.

## Access Control Model

The access control is based on roles: `AgentCreator`, `Admin`, `Editor`, `Viewer`, and `Audience`.

**JSON property names:** The Viewer-role claim list is named `users` in API v1 request and response bodies. API v2 uses `viewers`.

- `AgentCreator` is a system-wide role that gives a privilege to create new agents.
  Typically this role is assigned to users based on the licenses they have. There are several license providers supported (EMS integration is coming soon).
  All Trimble employees are automatically assigned a license that gives this role (well-known license id with full access for Trimble employees is `00581fef-acde-4f9a-8a25-e05f529e8cb5`).

- `Admin` is a role in the scope of a specific "agent".
  Actors with this role can configure the agents, delete agent, manage agent's permissions. The `Admin` can also do everything that `Editor` and `Viewer` can do in the resource scope.

- `Editor` is a role in the scope of a specific "agent".
  Actors with this role can view all agent configurations, update non-ACL
  agent configuration, run validation checks, create and manage their own
  threads, and view anonymized runs. Editors cannot
  change ACLs or delete the agent.

- `Viewer` is a role in the scope of a specific "agent".
  Actors with `Viewer` role can interact with the agent by sending prompts and receiving completions (this includes also the searching in the index the agent is connected to even if the actor does not have a direct permission to see the index).

- `Audience` is a role derived from alias audience membership.
  Actors in an alias audience (e.g. the `published` alias) receive limited read access to the agent descriptor — only the agent `id`, `effectiveRoles`, and user-scoped configuration fields are returned (admin-level fields such as access lists, timestamps, and version are omitted). Audience actors also have thread and run permissions identical to the `Viewer` role. The `Audience` role is not assigned via ACLs; it is computed automatically from the alias audience lists.

Role is a collection of permissions. Following is a full list of permissions supported.

- `agents:create` - Create new agent
- `agents:read:full` - View all full agent configuration
- `agents:read:limited` - View limited properties of the agent
- `agents:report` - Get reports on agent usage by other users
- `agents:update` - Update agent configuration excluding ACL changes
- `agents:control` - Change agent ACL entries such as admins, editors, and viewers
- `agents:delete` - Delete agent (both soft and hard delete)
- `agents:validate` - Run validation checks for agent configuration
- `threads:create` - Create new thread
- `threads:read` - Read existing thread properties
- `threads:read:all` - Read anonymized runs across all users (thread-level analytics)
- `threads:update` - Update properties of the thread (like `name`)
- `threads:delete` - Delete thread
- `runs:create` - Create a new run in a thread
- `runs:read` - Read the run state

Following table lists permissions that are part of each role

| Role               | Permissions included in the role                                                                                                                                                    |
| ------------------ | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `AgentCreator`     | `agents:create`                                                                                                                                                                     |
| `Admin`            | `agents:control`, `agents:delete`, `agents:report` + all permissions from `Editor` role                                                                                             |
| `Editor`           | `agents:read:full`, `agents:update`, `agents:validate`, `threads:read:all` + all permissions from `Viewer` role                                                                  |
| `Viewer`           | `agents:read:full`, `threads:create`, `threads:read`, `threads:update`, `threads:delete`, `runs:create`, `runs:read`                                                             |
| `Audience`         | `agents:read:limited`, `threads:create`, `threads:read`, `threads:update`, `threads:delete`, `runs:create`, `runs:read`                                                             |

Actors are assigned roles in the scope of specific resources using claims that refer to various information associated with the actor.
Supported claims are:

- `users:<id>` - TID user identity, present if actor identity type is `user` (source is TID JWT `sub` claim)
- `applications:<id>` - TID application identity, present if actor identity type is `application` (source is TID JWT `sub` claim)
- `accounts:<id>` - user's IAM account identifier (source is TID JWT `account_id` claim)
- `trimble-employee:true|false` - indicates whether actor belongs to Trimble organization (TID JWT `amr` claim and IAM is a source of this information)
- `trimble-automation:true|false` - indicates whether actor is Trimble test automation user (TID JWT `amr` claim is a source of this information)
- `licenses-aaip:<id>` - the identifier of license provided by AAIP the actor has

A special `*` value can be used with `users:`, `applications:` and `licenses-aaip:` claim types to refer to any authenticated user, application or presence of a AAIP license.

For example

- `users:123456789` refers to the user with TID id `123456789`.
- The `applications:*` refers to any authenticated application.
- The `accounts:987` refers to any user from IAM account `987`.
- The `trimble-employee:true` claim present for all Trimble employees.
- The `trimble-automation:true` claim present for all Trimble automation user accounts.

### Examples of agent roles assignments

The JSON below uses the API v1 shape. The `users` property carries the Viewer-role claim list; API v2 exposes the same data under `viewers`.

1. The agent owned by user with id `123`, open for all Trimble employees to use.

    ```json
    {
      "admins": ["users:123"],
      "users": ["trimble-employee:true"]
    }
    ```

2. The agent owned by user with id `123`, open for any TID authenticated user and application actors.

    ```json
    {
      "admins": ["users:123"],
      "users": ["users:*", "applications:*"]
    }
    ```

3. The agent owned by users with id `123` and `987`, open for use by application with id `777`.

    ```json
    {
      "admins": ["users:123", "users:987"],
      "users": ["applications:777"]
    }
    ```

4. The agent owned by user with id `123`, with user `456` as an editor who can test the agent and view all configurations, and open for all Trimble employees to use.

    ```json
    {
      "admins": ["users:123"],
      "editors": ["users:456"],
      "users": ["trimble-employee:true"]
    }
    ```

5. The agent with all members of the same IAM account being editors who can evaluate agent behavior and change the agent configuration without controlling agent visibility (sharing).

    ```json
    {
      "admins": ["users:123"],
      "editors": ["accounts:555"],
      "users": ["users:*"]
    }
    ```

## Search, Filtering and Autocompletion

Service supports full text fuzzy text search for agents, threads, and runs.

The search and autocomplete endpoints support searching across multiple resource types at the same time or for a specific resource type.

Search endpoint is optimized for search quality, while autocomplete endpoint is optimized for the performance and might miss some results that would be included in the full search response. Autocomplete endpoint also has less support for query parameters prioritizing the ability to quickly return the most relevant results.

**Important:** When searching for agent resources, translation tables are NOT searched, only the default `name` and `description` values. Same with sorting and filtering, translated values are NOT taken into account.

### Filtering

Filters allow to narrow down results of the listing and search operations. Common filters are supported across several endpoints.
The filter query follows the [FIQL (Feed Item Query Language](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-nottingham-atompub-fiql-00) syntax.

#### Query operators

The following table lists the valid query operators:

| Operator    | Usage                                                                                               |
| ----------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `==`        | Equals. Matches if field value equals the specified value.                                          |
| `!=`        | Not equals. Matches if field value does not equal the specified value.                              |
| `=gt=`      | Greater than. Matches if field value is greater than the specified value (datetime fields).         |
| `=ge=`      | Greater than or equal. Matches if field value is greater than or equal to the specified value.      |
| `=lt=`      | Less than. Matches if field value is less than the specified value (datetime fields).               |
| `=le=`      | Less than or equal. Matches if field value is less than or equal to the specified value.            |
| `=in=`      | In list (any-of). Comma-separated values use OR semantics: `field=in=(val1,val2)` matches if the field contains **any** of the values. Semicolon-separated values use AND semantics: `field=in=(val1;val2)` matches if the field contains **all** of the values. |
| `=out=`     | Not in list. Comma-separated values exclude **any**: `field=out=(val1,val2)` matches if the field contains **none** of the values. Semicolon-separated values exclude **all**: `field=out=(val1;val2)` matches if the field is missing **at least one** of the values. |
| `;` (AND)   | Logical AND. Combines multiple conditions where all must match: `field1==val1;field2==val2`.        |
| `,` (OR)    | Logical OR. Combines multiple conditions where at least one must match: `field1==val1,field2==val2`.|

**Note on wildcards:** While the FIQL specification allows wildcard patterns using `*` (e.g., `name==*test*`), this implementation treats `*` as a literal character, not as a wildcard pattern. For fuzzy or partial matching, use the search or autocomplete endpoints instead of filters.

**Encoding special characters:** Space characters in FIQL filter values must be percent-encoded as `%20` according to [RFC 3986](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3986). Since FIQL queries are passed as URI query parameters, the percent sign must then be encoded again for HTTP transport, resulting in `%2520` for spaces.

For example, to filter by the name "My Agent":

- FIQL expression: `name==My%20Agent`
- In the HTTP URI query string: `?filter=name==My%2520Agent`

The server decodes both layers: first the HTTP transport decoding (restoring `%20`), then the FIQL decoding (restoring the space character).

**Datetime format:** Use ISO 8601 format with timezone: `2024-01-15T12:10:30.000000Z`.

#### Query terms (selectors)

##### Agent selectors

The following table lists all valid `agents` query terms. Fields marked as **admin-only** will only return results where the current user is an admin of the agent.

| Selector         | Type     | Valid operators                            | Admin-only | Usage                                                                                                     |
| ---------------- | -------- | ------------------------------------------ | ---------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `name`           | string   | `==`, `!=`                                 | No         | Filter by agent name. Use URL encoding for spaces: `name==My%20Agent`.                                    |
| `title`          | string   | `==`, `!=`                                 | No         | Alias for `name`. Useful in combined agent/thread searches.                                               |
| `tags`           | array    | `==`, `!=`, `=in=`, `=out=`                | No         | Filter by tags. Matching is **case-sensitive**. `tags==production` for exact match, `tags=in=(a,b)` for any-of, `tags=in=(a;b)` for all-of. |
| `createdAt`      | datetime | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=` | Yes        | Filter by creation timestamp.                                                                             |
| `updatedAt`      | datetime | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=` | Yes        | Filter by last update timestamp.                                                                          |
| `accessUpdatedAt`| datetime | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=` | Yes        | Filter by last access control update timestamp.                                                           |
| `createdBy`      | string   | `==`, `!=`, `=in=`, `=out=`                | Yes        | Filter by creator. Value format: `users:{userId}` or `applications:{applicationId}`.                              |
| `updatedBy`      | string   | `==`, `!=`, `=in=`, `=out=`                | Yes        | Filter by last updater. Value format: `users:{userId}` or `applications:{applicationId}`.                         |
| `accessUpdatedBy`| string   | `==`, `!=`, `=in=`, `=out=`                | Yes        | Filter by last access control updater. Value format: `users:{userId}` or `applications:{applicationId}`.          |
| `ownerAccountId` | string   | `==`, `!=`, `=in=`, `=out=`                | Yes        | Filter by owner account ID.                                                                               |
| `admins`         | array    | `==`, `!=`, `=in=`, `=out=`                | Yes        | Filter by admin membership. Value format: `users:{userId}`, `accounts:{accountId}` or any other supported claim.                     |
| `editors`        | array    | `==`, `!=`, `=in=`, `=out=`                | Yes        | Filter by editor membership. Value format: `users:{userId}`, `accounts:{accountId}` or any other supported claim.                    |
| `sharedWith`     | array    | `==`, `!=`, `=in=`, `=out=`                | Yes        | Filter by actors able to interact with the agent regardless of their role (`admins`, `editors`, `viewers`, or alias audiences), i.e., anyone the agent is shared with in any capacity. Value format: `users:{userId}`, `accounts:{accountId}` or any other supported claim.          |
| `effectiveRoles` | special  | `=in=`                                     | N/A        | Context-sensitive filter. Supports values `admin`, `editor`, `viewer`, and `<slug>.audience` (e.g. `published.audience`). Returns agents where the current user has the specified role(s). Multiple values can be combined: `effectiveRoles=in=(admin,editor,viewer)`. Alias audience roles use the format `<aliasSlug>.audience` and match when the current user is in the audience of the named alias. |

##### Thread selectors

The following table lists all valid `threads` query terms.

| Selector    | Type     | Valid operators                            | Usage                                                                              |
| ----------- | -------- | ------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `title`     | string   | `==`, `!=`                                 | Filter by thread title. Use URL encoding for spaces: `title==My%20Thread`.         |
