| `name`      | string   | `==`, `!=`                                 | Alias for `title`. Useful in combined agent/thread searches.                       |
| `createdAt` | datetime | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=` | Filter by creation timestamp.                                                      |
| `updatedAt` | datetime | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=` | Filter by last update timestamp.                                                   |
| `agentId`   | string   | `==`, `!=`, `=in=`, `=out=`                | Filter by parent agent ID (autocomplete/search only).                              |

##### Run selectors

The following table lists all valid `runs` query terms.

| Selector    | Type     | Valid operators                            | Usage                                                                              |
| ----------- | -------- | ------------------------------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `createdAt` | datetime | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=` | Filter by creation timestamp.                                                      |
| `threadId`  | string   | `==`, `!=`, `=in=`, `=out=`                | Filter by parent thread ID (search only).                                          |
| `thread.createdAt` | datetime | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=` | Filter by thread creation timestamp.                                        |
| `status`    | string   | `==`, `!=`                                 | Filter by status of the run. Possible values: `inProgress`, `suspended`, `error`, `cancelled`, `finished`.|
| `error.code`| string   | `==`, `!=`                                 | Filter by error code. |
| `feedbacks` | array    | `==`, `!=`                                 | Filter by presence or absence of feedback. Can be compared only to `null`.         |
| `feedbacks.type`| int  | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=` | Filter by type of feedback. Positive feedback and negative feedback.               |
| `feedbacks.comment`| string | `==` , `!=`                           | Filter by presence or absence of comment in the feedback. Can be compared only to `null`. Typically used in combination as `feedbacks!=null;feedbacks.comment==null`|

**Note on cross-resource filtering:** When using the combined search/autocomplete endpoints that return multiple resource types (agents, threads, runs), filter terms that are only applicable to one resource type will evaluate to FALSE for the other types. For example, filtering by `admins` (agent-only) will not return any threads or runs. If combined with OR conditions, the applicable constraints will still be evaluated.

#### Query string examples

**Basic equality:**

```
filter=name==MyAgent
```

**String with spaces (URL-encoded):**

```
filter=name==My%20Test%20Agent
```

**Datetime comparison (agents created after a date):**

```
filter=createdAt=gt=2024-01-01T00:00:00Z
```

**Datetime range (agents created between two dates):**

```
filter=createdAt=ge=2024-01-01T00:00:00Z;createdAt=lt=2024-02-01T00:00:00Z
```

**Filter by actors who can interact with the agent (regardless of their role):**

```
filter=sharedWith=in=(users:12345)
```

**Filter by multiple admins:**

```
filter=admins=in=(users:111,users:222,accounts:333)
```

**Combine conditions with AND:**

```
filter=name==MyAgent;createdAt=gt=2024-01-01T00:00:00Z
```

**Combine conditions with OR:**

```
filter=name==AgentA,name==AgentB
```

**Find agents where current user is admin or editor:**

```
filter=effectiveRoles=in=(admin,editor)
```

**Find agents where current user is in the published alias audience:**

```
filter=effectiveRoles=in=(published.audience)
```

**Exclude specific agents by name:**

```
filter=name=out=(TestAgent,DemoAgent)
```

**Complex filter (name matches OR created by specific user AND updated recently):**

```
filter=name==MyAgent,(createdBy==users:12345;updatedAt=gt=2024-06-01T00:00:00Z)
```

**Filter by exact tag** (case-sensitive — `Production` and `production` are distinct values)**:**

```
filter=tags==production
```

**Filter by any of the specified tags (OR/any-of):**

```
filter=tags=in=(production,staging)
```

**Filter by all of the specified tags (AND/all-of):**

```
filter=tags=in=(production;internal)
```

**Combine tag filter with other selectors:**

```
filter=tags==production;name==MyAgent
```

## Using API Efficiently

Use endpoint capabilities to keep client-server communication efficient:

- Use `POST /v1/batch-get` when you need to fetch many resources by identifier in one round-trip, instead of sending many independent requests.
- Use list, search and autocomplete for discovery workflows, and use direct resource endpoints and the `POST /v1/batch-get` endpoint for detail retrieval.
- Use `fields` to request only the properties needed by the client to reduce payload size.
- Use pagination (`pageSize`) on collection endpoints and continue via `links.next.href`.
- Prefer targeted filtering and sorting to limit response size and reduce client-side processing.

## Reports

The Reports API provides analytics for agent owners. Reports are purpose-built endpoints that return aggregated metrics within a requested time range, scoped to a single agent.

Each report response includes:

- **Summary** — aggregate totals for the entire requested period
- **Time series** — periodic data points suitable for charting

### Common Parameters

All report endpoints share the following query parameters:

| Parameter | Type     | Description                                                                 |
| --------- | -------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `from`    | datetime | Start of the report period (inclusive, UTC). Normalized to midnight UTC.    |
| `to`      | datetime | End of the report period (inclusive, UTC). Normalized to midnight UTC.      |
| `metrics` | string   | Comma-separated list of metrics to include in the report.                   |

**Constraints:**

- The maximum report timeframe is **365 days**. Requests exceeding this limit are rejected with a 422 error.
- Date values are normalized to midnight UTC (00:00:00Z). Any time component in `from` or `to` is silently truncated.

### Runs Report Filtering

The runs report supports a FIQL `filter` parameter to narrow the aggregated data. The FIQL syntax is the same as described in the Filtering section above.

#### Runs report selectors

| Selector       | Type    | Valid operators        | Description                                                                                                  |
| -------------- | ------- | ---------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| `modelId`      | string  | `==`, `!=`, `=in=`, `=out=` | Filter runs by the LLM model used.                                                                           |
| `status`       | string  | `==`, `!=`, `=in=`, `=out=`     | Filter runs by status: `inProgress`, `suspended`, `error`, `cancelled`, `finished`.                          |
| `agentVersion` | integer | `==`, `!=`, `=gt=`, `=ge=`, `=lt=`, `=le=`, `=in=`, `=out=` | Filter runs by the agent version used to create them.                              |
| `messages.toolCalls.function.name` | string | `==`, `=in=`, `=out=`   | Filter runs to those that invoked the specified tool. The `toolCalls` metric counts only matching invocations.|

#### Example

Runs created with model `gpt-4.1-nano` that finished successfully:

```
filter=modelId==gpt-4.1-nano;status==finished
```

### Filter Values

The filter values endpoint (`/agents/{agentId}/reports/values`) returns the distinct values that exist for a given filter field across the agent's runs within the specified time range. This is useful for populating filter dropdowns in a UI before calling the runs report endpoint.

## Soft and Hard Delete, Data Retention

### Deleting and Deactivating (soft-delete) Agents

Only actors with the Admin role can delete an agent.
There are two levels of the "deletion" for agents: soft-delete and permanent delete.

The soft-delete state deactivates the agent and prevents any further workflow executions. The history (existing threads and runs) becomes inaccessible by default for soft-deleted agents. To access soft-deleted agents or threads from soft-deleted agents, use the `state` query parameter (see Common Query Parameters). The search endpoint does not respect the state parameter when searching threads.
By default, APIs return 404 when a soft-deleted resource is requested (or filter out such agents from lists). Specify `state=deleted` (or `state=active,deleted` for both) to access them. Restore a soft-deleted agent by PATCHing it with `state=deleted` in the query.

To delete a resource permanently, the delete endpoints accept `permanent=true` query parameter.
If this parameter is passed and the caller has corresponding permissions, the agent is deleted completely with all its history and metadata. The identifier of the agent becomes available to be used by a new resource.

### Deleting Threads

Users can delete their own threads. In this case, the thread is immediately deleted and not recoverable.

### Data Retention

Threads that were not active for more than 2 years are automatically and permanently hard-deleted.

## Error Handling

### Handling Errors and Retry Logic

Common HTTP Response Status Codes are used.
The response body for all error responses are in the same format and include the error code and message.

When you send requests to and get responses from the Service API, you might encounter two types of API errors:

- **Client errors** - Client errors are indicated by a 4xx HTTP response code. Client errors indicate
that the service found a problem with the client request. This can include such things as an authentication
failure or missing required parameters. Fix the issue in the client application before submitting the request again.
- **Server errors** - Server errors are indicated by a 5xx HTTP response code and need to be resolved by Trimble.
You may resubmit/retry the request until it succeeds.

For each API error, the API Gateway returns the following values:

- A status code, for example, `400`
- An error type, for example, `tag:BadRequest`
- An error message, for example, `Supplied AttributeValue is empty, must contain exactly one of the
supported data types`

### Error Response Body Schema

All error responses follow the same structure for the response body and are conformant to [RFC 9457](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9457.html) specification.
The error types use `tag:` URI schema and cannot be dereferenced.

| Error type                                | HTTP Status Code | Error description |
| ----------------------------------------- | --- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| `tag:BadRequest`                          | 400 | Bad request.|
| `tag:ConflictingHeaders`                  | 400 | Only one of If-Match or If-None-Match header is allowed in a request.|
| `tag:ContentFilter`                       | 400 | Content was filtered.|
| `tag:ContextLengthExceeded`               | 400 | The input exceeds the model's context window after trimming. Non-trimmable parts (system prompt, current message, run context) are too large for the configured model. Or output exceeds the maximum number of tokens set for the output.|
| `tag:InvalidAcl`                          | 400 | The ACL cannot be empty on space level.|
| `tag:InvalidIfMatchHeader`                | 400 | Invalid If-Match Header.|
| `tag:InvalidName`                         | 400 | The resource name is invalid.|
| `tag:InvalidPageSize`                     | 400 | The request contains invalid page size.|
| `tag:InvalidParentParam`                  | 400 | The parent param is invalid.|
| `tag:InvalidPayload`                      | 400 | One of id or name is mandatory. In case of a new file upload name is mandatory.|
| `tag:InvalidQueryParam`                   | 400 | Invalid query param: X.|
| `tag:InvalidSkipToken`                    | 400 | The request contains invalid skip token.|
| `tag:InvalidFilter`                       | 400 | Invalid FIQL filter syntax, unsupported field, or invalid value format.|
| `tag:InvalidSortParameter`                | 400 | The given sort parameter is invalid.|
| `tag:InvalidStateParam`                   | 400 | The state param is invalid.|
| `tag:InvalidUpload`                       | 400 | The file status is not UPLOADABLE.|
| `tag:InvalidParam`                        | 400 | Please provide a valid version.|
| `tag:PageSizeExceeded`                    | 400 | The pageSize parameter exceeds the maximum supported page size (N).|
| `tag:InvalidModel`                        | 400 | The selected model is not valid.|
| `tag:MissingContext`                      | 400 | Required context variable is missing from the request.|
| `tag:TurnLimitExceeded`                   | 400 | The number of turns in the run exceeded the limit set in agent configuration.|
| `tag:UnexpectedToolCall`                  | 400 | The LLM produced an unexpected tool call that is not part of the agent configuration for the run.|
| `tag:MalformedFunctionCall`               | 400 | The LLM produced a malformed function call.|
| `tag:TokenExchangeError`                  | 400 | Token exchange failed.|
| `tag:UnresolvedToolCalls`                 | 400 | There are assistant tool calls without corresponding tool results.|
| `tag:UnAuthorized`                        | 401 | The request is not authorized.|
| `tag:InvalidLicense`                      | 403 | The specified license is not available for this user.|

### Error Status Codes (Client and Server Errors)

HTTP status codes indicate whether an operation is successful or not.
A response code of 2xx indicates that the operation was successful. Other error codes indicate either a
client error (4xx) or a server error (5xx).
The error table lists the errors returned by the service. Some errors are resolved if you retry the same request.
As a general rule, the timeout errors, network errors, throttling errors, and 5xx errors may be retried.
The 401 error code usually indicates expired credentials and should be retried after refreshing the access token.
The error table indicates which errors are likely to be resolved with successive retries.

If the value of the Retry column is:

- **Yes** - Submit the same request again.
- **Yes if idempotent** - Submit the same request again, but only if the given method has been
implemented with idempotence.
- **No** - Fix the problem on the client-side before submitting a new request.

For more information about retrying requests, see Error Retries and Exponential Backoff.

| HTTP Status Code | Error type class                      | Retry                                             |
| ---------------- | ------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- |
| 400              |  Bad Request Exception                | No                                                |
| 401              |  Unauthorized Exception               | No, could be retried after acquiring a new token  |
| 403              |  Access Denied Exception              | No                                                |
| 404              |  Not Found Exception                  | No                                                |
| 406              |  Not Acceptable Exception             | No                                                |
| 409              |  Conflict Exception                   | No                                                |
| 412              |  Precondition Failed Exception        | No                                                |
| 422              |  Unprocessable Content Exception      | No                                                |
| 429              |  Too Many Requests Exception          | Yes                                               |
| 500              |  Internal Service Failure Exception   | Yes                                               |
| 502              |  Bad Gateway Exception                | Yes if idempotent                                 |
| 503              |  Service Unavailable Exception        | Yes                                               |
| 504              |  Endpoint Request Timed-out Exception | Yes if idempotent                                 |

### Catching Errors

For your application to run smoothly, you need to build in logic to catch and respond to errors.
One typical approach is to implement your request within a try block or if-then statement.
The client-side SDKs typically perform retries and error checking out of the box.

The error code and description are always sent in the request response body in addition to HTTP status code.
To troubleshoot the problem, please capture the request identifier using the `x-request-id`, `X-Azure-Ref`, `x-amzn-RequestId` HTTP response headers. Inform the value received in these headers to the support team.

### Error Retries and Exponential Backoff

Numerous components on a network, such as DNS servers, switches, load balancers,
and others can generate errors anywhere in the life of a given request.

The usual technique for dealing with these error responses in a networked environment
is to implement retries in the client application.
This technique increases the reliability of the application and reduces operational costs for the developer.

You should retry original requests that receive server errors (5xx).
However, client errors (4xx, other than a TooManyRequestsException) indicate you need to
revise the request itself to correct the problem before trying again.

In addition to simple retries, it is recommended to use an exponential backoff algorithm for better flow control.
The idea behind exponential backoff is to use progressively longer waits between retries
for consecutive error responses.
For example, you might let one second elapse
before the first retry, four seconds before the second retry, 16 seconds before the third retry, and so on.
However, if the request has not succeeded after a minute, the problem might be a hard limit and not the
request rate.
For example, you may have reached the maximum number of pipelines allowed. Set the maximum
number of retries to stop around one minute.

## Payload Compression

The service accepts `gzip` and `br` compressed request payloads. The `Content-Encoding` header must be sent in the request
to decode the request payload properly.

The service supports the following content encodings for responses:

- `gzip`
- `br`
- `identity`

Only responses larger than 1KB might be encoded. Small responses are sent without encoding.

The encoding (compression) method can be negotiated with the `Accept-Encoding` header.
The following `Accept-Encoding` header formats, according to the [RFC 7231](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#section-5.3.4) specification, are supported:

- `Accept-Encoding:br,gzip`
- `Accept-Encoding:` - If the Accept-Encoding header is present and empty, only the unencoded content is acceptable.
- `Accept-Encoding:*` - This is the default value if the header is not present. This directive does not suggest that any algorithm is supported but indicates that no preference is expressed. Service will decide on the best compression method.
- `Accept-Encoding:br;q=0.5,gzip=1.0`
- `Accept-Encoding:gzip;q=1.0,identity;q=0.5,*;q=0`

If the header is not set, the default value is `*` as defined in RFC 7231. In such cases, the payload is compressed with the best supported compression method.

## Localization

The Agent Service API supports localization of agents through the `translations` field in the agent configuration and the `Accept-Language` HTTP header.

### Translation Table

Agent configurations can include a `translations` field containing a dictionary of language-specific translations for the following properties:

- `name` - The agent name
- `description` - The agent description
- `samplePrompts` - Sample prompts shown to users
- `promptPlaceholder` - Placeholder text for the prompt input field

The translation table is a dictionary where keys are language tags (e.g., `en-US`, `fi`, `de-DE`) and values are objects containing any combination of the localizable properties listed above.

**Example translation table:**

```json
{
  "translations": {
    "fi": {
      "name": "Suomenkielinen assistentti",
      "description": "Apuohjelma joka auttaa suomeksi",
      "samplePrompts": [
        {"content": "Kerro minulle säästä"}
      ],
      "promptPlaceholder": "Kirjoita viestisi tähän"
    },
    "de-DE": {
      "name": "Deutschsprachiger Assistent",
      "description": "Ein Assistent der auf Deutsch hilft"
    }
  }
}
```

### Accept-Language Header

The `GET /agents` and `GET /agents/{agentId}` endpoints respect the `Accept-Language` HTTP header for content negotiation:

- If the `Accept-Language` header is **not present**, the API returns the default (non-translated) values for all properties
- If the `Accept-Language` header **is present**, the API attempts to find a matching language tag in the agent's translation table:
  - Language tags are matched using **case-insensitive exact string comparison**
  - The API follows the language preferences specified in the `Accept-Language` header in order of quality values (as defined in [RFC 7231](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#section-5.3.5))
  - No additional fallback rules are implemented (e.g., `en-US` will not automatically fall back to `en`)
  - Only properties present in the matched translation entry are replaced; other properties retain their default values

**Example Accept-Language headers:**

- `Accept-Language: fi` - Request Finnish translations
- `Accept-Language: en-US,en;q=0.9,fi;q=0.8` - Prefer US English, then English, then Finnish
- `Accept-Language: de-DE` - Request German (Germany) translations

### Content-Language Response Header

When a matching translation is found and applied, the API includes a `Content-Language` response header containing the matched language tag from the translation table.

- If no `Accept-Language` header is present: no `Content-Language` header is returned
- If `Accept-Language` is present but no matching translation exists: no `Content-Language` header is returned
- If a matching translation is found: `Content-Language` header contains the matched language tag (e.g., `Content-Language: fi`)

### Configuration Limits

- Maximum number of language entries in the translation table: 30
- Each translated property is subject to the same length constraints as the default property

## Context Engineering Best Practices

### System Prompt

The system prompt is prepended to every request sent to the LLM. Because it is included in every message, its size has a direct and compounding impact on context window consumption, token costs, and response latency.

**Keep the system prompt concise.** A shorter system prompt leaves more room in the context window for conversation history, tool results, and retrieved knowledge base content. This is especially important for agents that use RAG, tools, or subagents, because each of those features adds its own content to the context.

**Practical guidelines:**

- State the agent's role, behavioral rules, and output format requirements. Avoid repeating information the model already knows or information that could be provided through the run context.
- Move large reference material (lookup tables, examples, FAQ content) into a knowledge base and connect it via the retrieval configuration instead of embedding it in the system prompt.
- Test with the shortest prompt that produces acceptable output quality, then iterate.

**Enforced limit:** The service enforces a maximum system prompt size of **4000 tokens** as a best practice. The token count is calculated using the tokenizer for all models configured for the agent. Requests that exceed this limit for any model are rejected with the `tag:SystemPromptTooManyTokens` error (HTTP 422).

### Tool Use

System enforces a limit of 50 MCP servers as a best practice, but it does not limit the total number of tools coming from all the MCP severs.
Keep number of tools to the minimum, disable not needed tools using the tools configuration.

### Subagents

Subagents are one of the most effective techniques for managing context window pressure in complex agent workflows.

**The context window problem:** Every element of an agent interaction — the system prompt, conversation history, tool definitions, tool call results, and retrieved knowledge base content — competes for space in a single, finite context window. As a conversation grows or as tool results accumulate, the agent risks exceeding the window, which leads to truncated history, degraded reasoning quality, or outright failures. This is especially pronounced for agents that combine RAG retrieval, multiple MCP tool servers, and long multi-turn conversations.

**How subagents help:** Each subagent invocation creates a separate run with a clean context. The parent agent sends only a focused natural-language message describing the subtask — it does not forward its full conversation history, accumulated tool results, or retrieved documents. The subagent processes the subtask within its own context window and returns a concise result to the parent. This keeps both the parent and the subagent operating well within their respective context limits.

**Practical benefits:**

- **Isolation of concerns.** A research subtask that retrieves large documents from a knowledge base consumes context only in the subagent, not in the parent. The parent sees only the summarized answer.
- **Parallelizable workloads.** The LLM can delegate independent subtasks to different subagents, each with its own clean context, rather than processing everything sequentially in a single growing window.
- **Specialization.** Each subagent can have its own system prompt, tools, and retrieval configuration tuned for a narrow task. A smaller, more focused system prompt and toolset means less baseline context consumption per agent.
- **Scalable complexity.** Multi-step workflows that would exhaust a single agent's context (e.g., "research three topics, compare findings, and draft a report") become feasible when each research step runs in its own subagent context.

**Supported architectures:** The subagent system supports both common multi-agent patterns:

- **Hierarchical (supervisor).** A single orchestrator agent receives the user's request, breaks it into subtasks, and delegates each subtask to a specialized subagent. The orchestrator synthesizes the results and responds to the user. This is the most straightforward pattern — one parent coordinates the workflow and each subagent focuses on a narrow domain.
- **Swarm of agents.** Subagents can themselves have subagents, forming a mesh where any agent in the graph can delegate to peers. This enables decentralized collaboration: an agent that encounters a subtask outside its expertise routes it to a more suitable agent without returning control to a central coordinator. The maximum delegation depth of 3 guard prevents runaway chains while still allowing flexible multi-hop routing.

Both patterns benefit equally from the context isolation described above — each agent in the graph operates within its own clean context window regardless of the overall topology.

**Design guidelines:**

- Write clear subagent descriptions so the parent LLM knows when and why to delegate. The description is the primary signal the LLM uses to choose the right subagent.
- Keep the delegation depth shallow (maximum 3). Each level adds latency and a network hop. Prefer a flat supervisor with specialized peers over deeply nested chains when possible.
- Avoid passing large payloads in the delegation message. Let each subagent retrieve its own data from knowledge bases or tools rather than relying on the parent to pre-fetch and forward content.
- Use `overrides.description` to give the parent LLM task-specific context about what the subagent does, independent of the subagent's own generic description.

## Limits

The service has the following limits:

- Maximum agent name length - 64
- Maximum agent description length - 2048
- Maximum system prompt size - 4000 tokens
- Maximum user message content size - 100000 characters
- Maximum number of knowledge base libraries connected to the single agent - 10
- Maximum number of tools in the agent configuration - 100
- Maximum number of subagents per agent configuration - 10
- Maximum subagent delegation depth - 3
- Maximum number of local tools that can be passed to a run - 100
- Maximum number of requested items per resource type in `POST /v1/batch-get` - 100
- context window size depends on the LLM configured for the agent
- Maximum size of the data URI for agent icon - 1 MB (corresponds to approximately 700 KB binary image size)
- Maximum number of tags per agent - 50
- Maximum tag length - 64 characters

The `feedbacks.type=lt=0` condition will match runs with at least one negative feedback.

To resume the existing suspended run, pass both `threadId` and `runId` in the request body. This is typically used to pass back the tool execution result or for the human in a loop interaction.

Response is returned as a stream of events (each event is an HTTP chunk). Refer to [AG UI Protocol](https://docs.ag-ui.com/introduction) for details.

For the agents that support multiple LLM models, the specific model can be selected by passing the `{ description: "modelId", value: <modelId> }` context item in the request's `context` array with the value equal to the model identifier to use for this specific run. If multiple such items are present, the one occurring first in the array is used.

If quotas are configured for the agent and the request would exceed the quota limits, a 429 error response is returned with a `Retry-After` header indicating the number of seconds to wait before retrying.

post.description:
Batch get resources (agents, threads, agent configs) by identifier in one request. Prefer this endpoint over many independent resource reads when the client already knows the identifiers.

Response behavior:
- Returns HTTP 200 for a valid batch request.
- Per-item outcomes are returned via `responses` and `errors`.
- Missing items are omitted from `responses`.
- Duplicate item identifiers are deduplicated in the result.

The `state` query parameter controls whether active and/or deleted agents are considered. The `fields` query parameter supports typed prefixes (for example `agents.description` or `agentConfigs.description`) to request non-core fields. Core fields are always returned.

Items are fetched in parallel to minimize latency. Response order is not guaranteed.

get.description:
Get the per-user usage report for an agent. Helps agent owners manage per-user usage quotas by returning percentile statistics of run counts and token consumption **per user** within the specified time range.

To calculate quota utilization percentages, compare numbers returned in the report with the configured usage quota limits.

The report timeframe must not exceed 365 days. The `granularity` parameter controls the time series bucket size and accepts any ISO 8601 duration that is at least 1 hour and resolves to a whole number of hours (e.g. PT1H, PT6H, P1D, P3D, P1W, P1DT6H). Default is P1D (daily).

Only the metrics listed in the `metrics` parameter are included in the response. The time series is zero-filled so every bucket in the requested range has an entry.

Reports are available only to agent admins and editors.

delete.description:
Mark an agent as deleted (not active). Soft deleted agents are not returned in the list of agents and cannot be used for prompting. If `permanent=true` is specified, the agent and all related threads are hard deleted immediately instead of marking them as not active (soft delete). Hard deletion is irreversible. Soft deletion is reversible by updating the agent with `state=deleted` query parameter.

Supports optimistic concurrency via `If-Match` header. When provided, the delete succeeds only if the ETag matches the current agent version; otherwise a `412 Precondition Failed` is returned.

post.description:
Triggers a new agent run (AKA workflow execution) or resumes an existing run.

To continue an existing thread (conversation) pass `threadId` in the request body, otherwise a new thread (conversation) is created.
To resume the existing suspended run, pass both `threadId` and `runId` in the request body. This is typically used to pass back the tool execution result or for the human in a loop interaction.

Response is returned as a stream of events (each event is an HTTP chunk). Refer to [AG UI Protocol](https://docs.ag-ui.com/introduction) for details.

For the agents that support multiple LLM models, the specific model can be selected by passing the `{ description: "modelId", value: <modelId> }` context item in the request's `context` array with the value equal to the model identifier to use for this specific run. If multiple such items are present, the one occurring first in the array is used.

If quotas are configured for the agent and the request would exceed the quota limits, a 429 error response is returned with a `Retry-After` header indicating the number of seconds to wait before retrying.

post.description:
Batch get resources (agents, threads, agent configs) by identifier in one request. Prefer this endpoint over many independent resource reads when the client already knows the identifiers.

Response behavior:
- Returns HTTP 200 for a valid batch request.
- Per-item outcomes are returned via `responses` and `errors`.
- Missing items are omitted from `responses`.
- Duplicate item identifiers are deduplicated in the result.

For agent items on `/v2/batch-get`, the `fields` query parameter uses the same selector contract as GetAgentV2/ListAgentsV2 (for example `access`, `license`, or `configs.latest.name`). Thread and agent-config items keep the existing batch-get field selection semantics. Core fields are always returned.

Items are fetched in parallel to minimize latency. Response order is not guaranteed.

get.description:
Get the per-user usage report for an agent. Helps agent owners manage per-user usage quotas by returning percentile statistics of run counts and token consumption **per user** within the specified time range.

To calculate quota utilization percentages, compare numbers returned in the report with the configured usage quota limits.

The report timeframe must not exceed 365 days. The `granularity` parameter controls the time series bucket size and accepts any ISO 8601 duration that is at least 1 hour and resolves to a whole number of hours (e.g. PT1H, PT6H, P1D, P3D, P1W, P1DT6H). Default is P1D (daily).

Only the metrics listed in the `metrics` parameter are included in the response. The time series is zero-filled so every bucket in the requested range has an entry.

Reports are available only to agent admins and editors.

allowedBaseUrls.title: Allowedbaseurls
allowedBaseUrls.description:
Optional security guardrail that restricts which URLs the curl tool is allowed to call. When set and non-empty, every tool call URL must match at least one entry as a prefix. Each entry is a URL whose scheme, host, port, and path are used as the prefix; query strings, fragments, and userinfo (`user:pass@`) in entries are not allowed.

Matching rules:
- Scheme and host are compared case-insensitively.
- Ports are compared after applying protocol defaults (80 for http, 443 for https), so `https://example.com/x` and `https://example.com:443/x` are equivalent.
- Paths are compared on a path-segment boundary: allowed entry `/v1` admits `/v1` and `/v1/anything` but NOT `/v1foo`.
- `.` and `..` segments in the tool-call URL are normalized before matching, so a call cannot use `/v1/../admin` to escape an allowed `/v1` prefix.

When this list is unset or empty, the curl tool may call any public http(s) endpoint permitted by the service-wide SSRF guard. For production agents it is strongly recommended to set `allowedBaseUrls` to the smallest set of upstream prefixes the agent needs.

headers.title: Headers
headers.description:
Static HTTP headers to send with every curl tool call. Use this to attach static values (e.g. an API key) or to inject authentication by configuring an `Authorization` header whose value is a token placeholder. Header values support placeholder substitution using [format string syntax](https://docs.python.org/3/library/string.html#format-string-syntax):
- `{actorToken?scopes=<scopes>}` - On-behalf-of token derived from the original token that initiated the request to the agent, using the TID Token Exchange flow with the specified scopes.
- `{actorTokenRaw}` - Original token used to initiate the request to the agent (not recommended, see security notes in the API docs).
- `{run.context.<description>}` - Context variable value where `<description>` is the description field of the context item provided in the run request.

Unlike MCP tools, the `{agentToken...}` placeholder is NOT supported for the curl tool: because the curl tool can target arbitrary HTTP URLs, injecting an agent (client credentials) token would let a prompt-injected model reach any upstream endpoint that trusts the agent beyond what the original actor authorized. Configure an MCP tool instead if agent-as-itself authentication is required.

Agent-level headers can be overridden per tool call by specifying the same header name (case-insensitive) in the tool call arguments.

allowedBaseUrls.title: Allowedbaseurls
allowedBaseUrls.description:
Optional security guardrail that restricts which URLs the curl tool is allowed to call. When set and non-empty, every tool call URL must match at least one entry as a prefix. Each entry is a URL whose scheme, host, port, and path are used as the prefix; query strings, fragments, and userinfo (`user:pass@`) in entries are not allowed.

- Scheme and host are compared case-insensitively.
- Ports are compared after applying protocol defaults (80 for http, 443 for https), so `https://example.com/x` and `https://example.com:443/x` are equivalent.
- Paths are compared on a path-segment boundary: allowed entry `/v1` admits `/v1` and `/v1/anything` but NOT `/v1foo`.
- `.` and `..` segments in the tool-call URL are normalized before matching, so a call cannot use `/v1/../admin` to escape an allowed `/v1` prefix.

When this list is unset or empty, the curl tool may call any public http(s) endpoint permitted by the service-wide SSRF guard. For production agents it is strongly recommended to set `allowedBaseUrls` to the smallest set of upstream prefixes the agent needs.

value.title: Value
value.description:
The value of the header to be sent. Uses [format string syntax](https://docs.python.org/3/library/string.html#format-string-syntax) to allow field substitution, e.g. `{actorToken}` inject the on-behalf-of token derived from the original token that initiated the request to the agent.

It is important to understand what scopes are required to be present in the token for the tool to be used. Each MCP tool has its own scopes requirements. E.g.
- to use the knowledge base tools, the `kb` scope is required.
- to use the N8N based MCP servers, the `Agentic-N8N-Webhook` scope is required.

For MCP tools, the following placeholders are supported:

**Token placeholders:**
- `{actorToken?scopes=<scopes>}` - On-behalf-of token derived from the original token that initiated the request to the agent. This is the recommended option in most cases. The `scopes` parameter specifies which OAuth scopes to request for the token. Note that the TID Token Exchange flow at the time of writing requires the `scopes` parameter and will fail without it. If the `scopes` are not provided or invalid, the agent will fail with a `tag:BadAgentConfiguration` error upon attempted usage. Examples: `{actorToken?scopes=kb}`, `{actorToken?scopes=kb Agentic-N8N-Webhook}`
- `{agentToken?scopes=<scopes>}` - Agent (client credentials) token created using service app credentials with the specified scopes. Note that the TID Client Credentials flow at the time of writing requires the `scopes` parameter and will fail without it. If the `scopes` are not provided or invalid, the agent will fail with a `tag:BadAgentConfiguration` error upon attempted usage. Examples: `{agentToken?scopes=kb}`, `{agentToken?scopes=kb Agentic-N8N-Webhook}`
- `{actorTokenRaw}` - Original token used to initiate the request to the agent. This option is ***not recommended*** as the token can expire unpredictably preventing successful tool execution and has problems with agent identity propagation. Also using this token requires that all the scopes needed for the tool are present in the original token, meaning known to the application that will be used to get the token.

**Context placeholders:**
- `{run.context.<description>}` - Context variable value where `<description>` is the description field of the context item provided in the run request. E.g. if context contains `{description: "product", value: "MyApp"}`, then `{run.context.product}` will be replaced with `MyApp`. Note that the context value is not suitable for passing secrets, tokens, or other sensitive data.

Optional overrides for this tool: input schema (per-argument JSON Schema fragments) and/or description shown to the model. 

Be careful when overriding tool schemas as the MCP server may not be able to handle the new schema. Consider the consequences of the overrides on the tool's functionality. Use the `/agents/{agentId}/validate` endpoint to check the resulting schema. 

Overrides can use [format string syntax](https://docs.python.org/3/library/string.html#format-string-syntax) as placeholders resolved in runtime.

connectionString.title: Connectionstring
connectionString.description: The connection string to the knowledge base. Includes the knowledge base url and query parameters including a set of libraries to search in. The search type can be `hybrid`, `semantic`, or `full_text`. The default search type is `hybrid`. Additional optional parameters: `useMongoReranking` (boolean), `numCandidates` (integer), `semanticWeight` (float), `fulltextWeight` (float), `filter` (FIQL metadata filter), `auth` (`actor` or `agent`). The `auth` parameter controls which token is used when connecting to the knowledge base: `actor` (default) uses the calling user's on-behalf-of token, while `agent` uses the agent application token (client credentials). For security, retrieval endpoints are restricted to `https://kb.dev.trimble-ai.com`, `https://kb.stage.trimble-ai.com`, and `https://kb.ai.trimble.com`. Supports `{run.context.<description>}` placeholder substitution for dynamic filtering based on run request context. Optional `filter` uses FIQL to limit the search scope by metadata (e.g. document tags, source.createdAt, source.contentType, source.size, etc.).Refer to the [Knowledge Base documentation](https://developer.dev.trimble-ai.com/api/kb#tag/retrieval/POST/v1/search) for a full list of supported metadata and filter syntax.E.g. `https://kb.dev.trimble-ai.com/v1/search?libraries=libraryId1,libraryId2&searchType=semantic&filter=tags=in=(category:{run.context.category};version:2025;lang:en)`.

queryRewriterPrompt.title: Queryrewriterprompt
queryRewriterPrompt.description: Conversational query rewriter prompt template. This template is used to generate a search query based on the thread history. Uses [format string syntax](https://docs.python.org/3/library/string.html#format-string-syntax) to allow field substitution, e.g. `{chat_history}` and `{question}` can be used to inject the chat thread history and the user's most recent question respectively.

top.title: Top
top.description: Number of documents to retrieve from the knowledge base to include in the grounding knowledge

scoreThreshold.title: Scorethreshold
scoreThreshold.description: Minimum similarity score that is required for the document to be included in the grounding knowledge.

queryRewriterTemperature.title: Queryrewritertemperature
queryRewriterTemperature.description: Temperature for the query rewriter LLM. Controls the creativity of the query rewriting. Lower temperature leads to more deterministic query rewriting, higher temperature leads to increased creativity. Defaults to 0 for consistent query rewriting.

queryRewriterSeed.title: Queryrewriterseed
queryRewriterSeed.description: The seed used for the query rewriter LLM random number generator. This is used to ensure that the same input will always produce the same query rewrite. If provided, must be greater or equal to 1.

New discriminated item types may appear in future API versions; clients must ignore list entries they do not recognize without failing.

tools.title: Tools
tools.description: The list of local tools the agent can use. The agent will suspend run execution when a local tool call is needed and pass control to the client. This list is combined with the tools defined in the agent configuration. Server-side tools from the agent configuration are executed without suspending the workflow run; the client is notified of their results via events.

context.title: Context
context.description:
Additional context to be passed to the agent. The context is used to provide additional information to the agent that is not part of the thread history. E.g. `product` and `version of the product` to narrow down the knowledge base retrieval.

**Note:** The context value is not suitable for passing secrets, tokens, or other sensitive data. Furthermore, each context item must have a unique `description` field. Duplicate descriptions are not allowed as they would cause conflicts during placeholder substitution in system prompts and tool configurations.

There are well known context objects that are handled by the agent in a special way:
- `modelId` - context object with this `description` is the LLM model identifier to use for the run. If not provided, the default model from the agent configuration is used. Supported only for the agents that are configured to use multiple models.

name.title: Name
name.description: Human readable name of the agent.

description.title: Description
description.description: A human-readable description of the agent. Used to assist users and other agents in understanding what the agent can do.

iconUrl.title: Iconurl
iconUrl.description: The url to the icon/thumbnail for the agent. The url can point to the external web resource, in this case the resource should be publicly accessible. Or it could be the inlined image expressed as a [data URL scheme](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc2397). The input inlined image is automatically center-cropped to a square, downscaled to 256x256 pixels if it is larger, and converted to a WebP format before being stored.

systemPrompt.title: Systemprompt
systemPrompt.description: Prompt to be supplied to the LLM as context on every message. The system prompt is used to provide instructions on how it should behave. The system prompt is limited to 4000 tokens (counted using the tokenizer of the configured models for the agent).

samplePrompts.title: Sampleprompts
samplePrompts.description: Sample prompts to give examples to the end user.

promptPlaceholder.title: Promptplaceholder
promptPlaceholder.description: Text to show as a placeholder in the chat input field.

modalities.description: Modalities supported by the agent for the input and output.

capabilities.description: Skills supported and enabled for the agent. Skills are used to determine what features are available for the agent.

retrieval.title: Retrieval
retrieval.description: Knowledge bases to be used for RAG retrieval. Multiple retrieval configurations can be specified, typical use case is knowledge bases in multiple regions. If empty, the agent will not use RAG.

tools.title: Tools
tools.description:
A list of tools the agent is allowed to use.

New discriminated item types may appear in future API versions; clients must ignore list entries they do not recognize without failing.

subagents.title: Subagents
subagents.description: A list of subagent references the agent can delegate tasks to. Each subagent appears as a callable tool to the LLM.

models.title: Models
models.description: The list of allowed LLM models along with their configuration to be used by the agent. First model in the list is used as a default.

shortMemorySize.title: Shortmemorysize
shortMemorySize.description: Deprecated. Optional limit for the number of recent runs used from the thread history. When null (default, recommended), token-based context window trimming automatically manages conversation history based on the model's context window size and max_output_tokens, keeping total input within 70% of the available context window. When set to an integer, uses the legacy run-count based approach which may under-utilize the context window.

turnLimit.title: Turnlimit
turnLimit.description: The maximum number of turns allowed for the agent. A turn represents one LLM invocation cycle in the agent workflow (LLM call -> tool execution). This limit helps control costs and prevent infinite loops by stopping execution when the agent exceeds the specified number of turns. When the limit is reached, the agent run will terminate with a tag:TurnLimitExceeded error. Lower values provide tighter cost control but may prevent the agent from completing complex multi-step tasks.

propertyNames.description: The language tag in the format <language>-<REGION>. For example, en-US, en-UK, nl-NL, etc.

tags.title: Tags
tags.description: Free-form string tags for discovery, filtering, and organization. Each tag must be 1-64 characters. Maximum 50 tags per agent.

admins.title: Admins
admins.description: Actors that are allowed to manage the agent configuration. Use claims based syntax to specify the actors or categories of actors.

editors.title: Editors
editors.description: Actors that are allowed to test and evaluate the agent. Editors can view all configurations, create new configurations, and execute runs against any configuration version. Use claims based syntax to specify the actors or groups of actors.

users.title: Users
users.description: Actors granted Viewer-role access: allowed to chat with the agent or search the agent's index. Use claims based syntax to specify the actors or categories of actors.
